▫ Mainly relying on larger batch sizes and various parallelism strategies to push GPU utilization up.

  • Scheduling/orchestration layer

▫ In a high-throughput regime, the goal is 100% GPU utilization.

▫ Very different from a low-latency scenario (which needs headroom reserved for traffic spikes).

▫ You can schedule across multiple regions and machines to smooth out the overall demand curve, keeping every card as fully loaded as possible.

  • API design layer

▫ A long-running agent doesn't need a traditional chat-completion-style API — you can design an interface better suited to background/responses/async patterns, paired with batch and scheduling optimization on the backend.

Designing the whole stack this way can achieve cost savings on the order of "roughly 94% cheaper than Anthropic" (she shows a slide with the comparison).

Doubleword's product positioning (briefly)

She has a slide explaining how Doubleword productizes this trade-off:

  • Instead of offering just one endpoint, they offer multiple endpoints, each with a different cost/latency curve.

  • Customers can choose based on their specific use case: for example, open-core, background agents use the async endpoint and enjoy low price, high throughput; interactive interfaces choose the highly interactive, low-latency endpoint.

Closing TL;DR

She wraps up with a few final points (also the thesis of the talk):

  • Async/long-running agents will become a mainstream workload, and their inference characteristics are very different from traditional chat: enormous token usage, but insensitive to real-time responsiveness.

  • The whole problem can be abstracted as a token problem: ‎⁠number of tokens × cost per token⁠.

  • You can control token count through aggressive context management (compaction, clearing useless tool results, external memory, cache).

  • Open-source models are now "good enough," and are often better than proprietary models on "intelligence/price" ratio, letting you trade cheap tokens for more compute and better results.

  • In async, long-running scenarios, you can design the inference stack by "trading interactivity/latency for high throughput and low price," optimizing everything from hardware to API around that.

  • The cheaper inference gets, the more "test-time compute" you can give an agent, unlocking stronger, smarter long-running systems — which is exactly the direction Doubleword is betting on.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Judge the Judge: Building LLM Evaluators That Actually Work with GEPA

Mahmoud Mabrouk, Agenta AI

✕

This talk demonstrates how to use GEPA to tune an LLM-as-a-judge until it aligns with human annotations and becomes genuinely trustworthy, for use in both offline and online evaluation.

The talk opens with a classic disaster story: you casually drop a "hallucination-detection LLM-as-a-judge" into your observability platform, the numbers all look great, but customers keep saying the system is terrible — because this judge was never actually calibrated against real human annotations. To avoid an evaluation that's "confidently wrong," what he wants to do is use an evolutionary optimization method like GEPA to tune the LLM's scoring prompt until it aligns closely with human annotations.

He then walks through one real case throughout the talk: an airline customer-service agent (from Sierra's TowBench dataset), which has many tools and very complex policy rules. He first preprocesses the originally rather complex assertion labels into "one human summary label per conversation trace" — for example, "non-compliant, because it canceled directly without first confirming whether the refund policy was met." The dataset is roughly 600 examples, with a reasonably OK ratio of compliant to non-compliant, but he repeatedly stresses: the data is genuinely messy and was generated in a complex way — a hard case, but exactly the kind that's suited to testing GEPA.

When designing evaluation metrics, instead of using one vague "success score," he breaks the problem down through error analysis into several explicit error types — e.g., policy adherence, response style, whether information was correctly conveyed to the customer, whether tools were called correctly, and so on. Importantly, every metric uses a binary judgment (yes/no), avoiding a fuzzy 1–5 scale as much as possible, because even two humans can rarely agree perfectly on that kind of scale, let alone get an LLM to learn it.

He then demonstrates the annotation workflow: a subject matter expert (here, a customer-service domain expert) reviews conversations one by one, first writing free-text comments, then classifying errors into the categories above, and crucially always writing down the "why" behind each judgment. This "reasoning text" turns out to be critical both for teaching the LLM-as-a-judge the policy, and for letting GEPA reflect on and revise the prompt later.

With the data and annotations in place, he moves into the core of GEPA: a prompt-optimization framework similar to a genetic algorithm. The flow: start with a seed prompt, run one round of eval, ask an LLM to produce a reflection on the poorly performing cases, and from that propose a new prompt (a "mutation") — sometimes merging two prompts into one (a "merge"). Each round scores many candidate prompts against a batch of test data, then uses logic similar to a "Pareto frontier" to select a set of candidates that are each "best at different cases," preserving diversity rather than just looking at the average score. New prompts are then sampled and generated from this candidate set, iterating round after round until the compute budget runs out.

He uses the GEPA author's open-source library, which has an API called "optimize_anything": you feed in a "candidate" (i.e., the current judge-prompt combination) and supply an evaluator function. What the evaluator does is run the LLM-as-a-judge with the current candidate prompt and log the output, error type, reasoning, and so on, so GEPA can produce good reflections and new candidates from this "diagnostic information."

On implementation details, he first hand-designed a seed judge that's deliberately "naive but clearly biased": default to "compliant" unless it sees a clear reason to call it "non-compliant." The point of this is to keep the LLM from judging wildly, guessing at policy purely on instinct. This initial judge scores around 61% accuracy on the validation set, saying "compliant" almost every time — but that's actually a sensible starting point: at least it doesn't pretend to be smart while actually judging at random.

He then shows how he wrote the reflection prompt. Using GEPA's default reflection prompt didn't yield effective policy rules, so he rewrote his own, explicitly telling the reflecting LLM: you're looking at an airline customer-service scenario, and you should use "the current judge's verdict + human ground truth + the human-written reasoning" to infer better policy clauses — adding or restructuring rules, turning vague statements into concrete conditions, and so on. This "reflection template with a strong prior" was one of the keys to the performance gain.

Next he demonstrates the whole pipeline in a Jupyter notebook: load the processed TowBench data, split into train/validation (with no overlapping cases), define the evaluator, and call GEPA to run a few hundred iterations. This experiment ran for several hours and burned through a fair amount of token spend. He repeatedly stresses: first check with a small number of iterations whether the reflections and new prompts make sense, observe the candidates GEPA produces and how they perform on individual cases, and only then gradually scale up the compute — rather than blindly throwing a huge compute budget at it from the start.

In terms of results, GEPA pulled the judge's accuracy from a little over 60% up to around 74% on validation; recall and precision for "catching non-compliance" in particular improved markedly — originally it could barely catch any non-compliance, and afterward it could distinguish reasonably well. On the training set, GEPA's Pareto frontier even reached 100% coverage, in the sense that for every single training case, at least one candidate prompt judged it correctly. However, compressing this knowledge — spread across many candidates — into "a single prompt that's highly accurate across every case" is still hard, and remains one of the current method's bottlenecks.

He also shares plenty of failed experiments and practical lessons. For instance: using small models (GPT-4o mini, other nano-tier or older models) as the judge or refiner failed almost completely — they couldn't learn the complex policy; what worked best was using a stronger model for reflection and a somewhat weaker — but not too weak — model for the judge. He also tried stuffing the full agent policy documentation directly into the seed prompt, which actually made optimization harder, likely because it got stuck early in a complex local optimum, making it harder for GEPA to explore new rule combinations; leaving the explicit policy out and learning purely from annotated reasoning instead left much more room to optimize.

Finally, he talks about cost and best practices: these experiments are expensive to run (a single experiment can cost $200–300), so choose your model, batch size, and iteration count carefully; a one-time prompt optimization using a strong model, in exchange for using a cheaper model long-term in online eval afterward, is an investment that can be amortized. His recommended workflow: first accept that "data and annotation design" is the hardest and most important step, then run small-scale experiments on GEPA to observe the quality of reflections and candidates, and scale up gradually from there; meanwhile, use tooling (he uses his own open-source platform, Agenta) to instrument both traces and the evaluation process, to make debugging and tuning easier.

Overall, the core message of this talk is: if you want LLM-as-a-judge to drive eval and automated optimization, you can't just write a nice-looking scoring prompt and ship it — you need to design clear error types and binary metrics for your specific business problem, collect human annotations with reasoning attached, then use an evolutionary framework like GEPA to systematically "calibrate" your judge against that data, while diligently debugging and tuning the model and templates throughout the process.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Lessons From RL Systems That Looked Fine Until They Didn't

Aethon

✕

This talk is about, drawing on the speaker's hands-on experience, why reinforcement learning (RL) systems "look fine and then blow up in production," and how she later learned to design more stable systems.

  1. The core problem: it's not that the reward is wrong, it's that optimization goes "out of control"

The speaker works in a hedge-fund/quant-AI environment, putting models to work in markets where the money and the risk are both very real. Her conclusion: a lot of RL failures happen not because the reward was designed wrong, but because the "scope of optimization" and "behavioral boundaries" weren't designed properly:

A policy will hunt for boundary loopholes in the training environment, quietly drifting under an aggregate metric that still looks fine — until the market changes, the distribution shifts, or it hits a hard constraint (capital, risk, regulation), at which point it blows up.

So she focuses on structural, system-level controls, rather than just tacking penalties onto the reward.

  1. From research to production: a brutally strict funnel

At the fund, before a strategy or model can go live, it has to pass through an entire rigorous process:

  • Start from a hypothesis, with every experiment given clear hash/run anchoring, to make it easy to track and detect drift (e.g., backtest performance getting worse).

  • Run backtests, walk-forward tests (simulating tomorrow's and the day-after's world), and tier-1 risk gates.

  • Review a pile of statistics (Sharpe ratio, various risk metrics) — a single run might involve 200-plus models, and in the end only a tiny handful survive into production.

She stresses: in this setting, don't just chase making "a single model as good as possible" — instead, put a whole cohort of models into something like a tournament environment where they compete against each other, and filter out the fragile ones.

  1. Production isn't as "simple" as it sounds: stability, scalability, sustainability

She complains that a lot of people think "shipping it to infra" is the same as production, but in reality there are several dimensions you need to handle:

  • Scale and capital allocation: whether the strategy still works properly as you go from a single portfolio to different capital scales.

  • Sustainability: the strategy can't be entirely at the mercy of momentary emotion or the day's sentiment; sentiment is allowed to influence the strategy, but it can't blow up drastically every single day.

  • Reliability and continuous learning: once each day's end-of-day data comes in, you have to decide whether to fine-tune or just adjust inference, making sure the return curve doesn't go from 40% today to 0% tomorrow.

This often means fast retraining and adjustment overnight, but without redoing everything from scratch — you have to balance cost, stability, and responsiveness.

  1. World models + RL: but take only the part we need

She breaks the system into three layers: "world model → individual model → RL algorithm."

  • World model: defines the world environment, used to "auto-prompt" different scenarios, letting the system continuously simulate "what would happen if tomorrow looked like this."

  • But she doesn't put blind faith in world models themselves — most open-source world models are designed for games/vision, so her approach is to strip out the vision-related and other unnecessary parts entirely, keeping only the core structure useful for financial decision-making.

  • This way she can leverage other people's infra without being dragged down by unnecessary noise.

They also use the world model to solve the problem of "distilling a customized small model for each customer": instead of forcing all distillation to feed back into the main model every single day, they first compare "this small model vs. the main model" on backtest/forward-test performance inside the world model, and use that to decide whether it's needed, and at what frequency (e.g., switching to weekly re-distillation instead of daily).

  1. Lessons from failure: a plain RL policy too easily "lives only in today"

At first they only did "traditional RL + single-agent policy":

  • Give the policy a goal and a reward, and let it optimize.

  • The result: the agent does "policy optimization" that exploits the current environment, while the market is a world that keeps changing tomorrow and the day after.

  • They found: you can't let the agent live only in "today"; nor can it over-rely on the distant past or unrealistic predictions of the future.

Later, using world-model infra built by a former colleague, they abstracted the original RL results into a prompt, fed it into the world model, grew a strategy out of that world, and finally distilled it back down to customers and the live system.

  1. Quantitative results: world models plus the right distillation strategy work better

She showed a few charts (described verbally):

  • Plain offline-RL approach vs. adding a world model/union: alpha and Sharpe both improve significantly after adding the world model.

  • Initially they just played around with the world model plus image generation directly, and the result was too noisy, giving the worst performance.

  • After that, they "cut off the head," keeping only the core structure, and mixed in their own data with generated artifacts; in the end they found that only by fully stripping out the excess visual noise and keeping just what's needed did they get the best result (the red line).

  • Distillation went from "re-distilling every night" to "distilling weekly plus inference every night," paired with offline fine-tuning on weekends/at night — which turned out overall more stable and cheaper.

  1. Carrying this same thinking over to a "taste" system

She then jumps from finance to her own interests in music and oil painting, complaining that today's LLMs/recommendation systems all have the same "aesthetic" and fail to capture a person's real taste. She applies the same structure from before:

  • On the finance side, strict hard limits like variance ratios and risk thresholds are used.

  • A taste system, meanwhile, can use "softer" but still bounded metrics, such as "satisfaction variance" or "PP + PP10 mean drop":

▫ Suppose the songs you usually like all cluster around a certain "taste level" — when the system recommends something new, it can allow roughly 18 "basis points" of deviation above or below that average (she uses 18pp as an example), giving you a sense of novelty without dumping you into a genre you don't recognize at all.

▫ This is similar to risk preference: even an extremely conservative person won't suddenly be pushed into an ultra-high-risk asset.

She stresses: taste can be encoded — the key is how you control the magnitude of "exploration."

  1. The world model as a "personal world": the role of the knowledge graph

Back to the world model, but this time in the context of human taste:

  • The market is already a ready-made world; human life and preferences, by contrast, are much looser.

  • She suggests using a knowledge graph to construct a "personal world":

▫ Put all data about you into the knowledge graph, with each node carrying detailed structure.

▫ The world model takes this knowledge graph in as a giant prompt, and from that can understand the relationships between different nodes.

▫ After that, you can operate on subgraphs — for example, "only look at the intersection of music × art × fashion" — to build a specific sub-world.

  • This reduces hallucination, and makes every recommendation/decision traceable.
  1. Designing the RL agent plus recommendation layer: avoiding a "black-box policy" and cold start

She criticizes the typical approach: taking a big policy like OpenAI Gym as a black box, tuning parameters, and hoping it magically learns well. Her suggestion:

  • Treat these large, opaque policies as a baseline, mainly used to solve "cold start."

  • The real differentiation should be at the layer above: your world model, novelty factors, and the "scaffolding" built from users' historical behavior.

  • Novelty in particular: don't just do purely random exploration — instead, treat the user's historical preferences as a growing dataset, giving exploration a direction.

  • This way, without frequently retraining the world model, you can still make recommendations feel more and more "like you," while avoiding the trap of relying purely on an open-source black-box policy that never learns anything stable in the long run.

  1. Evaluation and constraints: agentic verdicts plus human-set "guardrails"

The final segment covers the evaluation/auditing part she cares about a great deal:

  • In a fund environment there's risk-management and audit pressure, but even in a personal system, you "should" still know what your agent is doing.

  • She designed an "evaluation agent":

▫ The RL agent still has tier-1 rewards.

▫ On top of that, an "agentic verdict" layer acts as a guardrail (she compares it to bowling-lane bumpers).

▫ These verdicts can be things like:

⁃ "Don't deviate more than ±18 basis points from the user's average taste."

⁃ "Don't surface certain types of content/pipeline."

▫ At heart these can be very simple JSON rules, set by humans and also trackable by the system.

She points out that without this kind of guardrail, RL will discover that some "extreme deviation" boosts engagement or satisfaction in the short term, and will keep charging in that direction — leaving the user ultimately feeling "this system doesn't understand me at all."

  1. Auditing and tracing that's actually usable: split into small files, weighted reading

She places a lot of weight on traces/logs, but knows most people are too lazy to read logs, so she shares a practical approach:

  • Don't have one giant 2,000–4,000-line log/cloud MD file.

  • Split the trace into several types of spec sheets, for example:

▫ A hypothesis spec (for researchers).

▫ A hash/back spec (a mapping from one encoding to another, similar to normalization).

▫ A walk-forward spec (in finance this might be "tomorrow's US stock market world"; in a taste system it could be new releases on Spotify, etc.).

▫ A tiers/gates rules file.

  • The production system can weight these spec sheets differently, so it's clear which layer's constraints or assumptions each decision came from.

  • This also lets the world model, the RL agent, and the evaluation agent trace against different files/nodes, instead of being drowned in a mass of noise.

She shows her own example: policy weighting handed to an agent called Sarah, with all ML flow and rollout rules centralized in an easily searchable interface (she also gives a nod to tools like Sky Portal, while noting that just JSON plus Slack works fine to start with).

To sum up, her main thesis is: to truly put a reinforcement learning system into production, the key isn't "designing the perfect reward function" — it's the boundary design of the whole system, world modeling, distillation strategy, and evaluation/audit structure. Having been forced to mature in a high-stakes financial setting, this approach can also be used to build "taste systems" and personalized agents that understand people better without spinning out of control.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Let's Teach Claude Code Semantic Code Search With turbopuffer

turbopuffer

✕

This talk explains how to equip Claude Code with a semantic code search system, implementing it with turbopuffer as a vector-plus-full-text search database and measuring the effect.

The speaker starts by clarifying a few terms: people often treat RAG as meaning "vector search only," but he stresses retrieval can actually be vector search, full-text search, or plain filtering; agentic search, likewise, isn't just grepping wildly — it means giving the agent a diverse toolkit so it can switch tools, change queries, and repeatedly narrow the scope when it can't find something.

He then contrasts Cursor and Claude Code. Cursor dumps every codebase (branch) into turbopuffer and does code search with "semantic search + grep," even designing a Merkle-tree-like structure so embeddings are only recomputed for files that changed on a branch, avoiding redundant cost. According to Cursor's internal experiments, adding semantic search lifts answer accuracy by about 12.5% on average, and by about 23.5% on their Composer model, which is specifically tuned for semantic search; real A/B tests also showed higher code retention and fewer unsatisfied requests on large codebases. By contrast, Claude Code currently relies only on grep-style tools, with no vector search.

He puts forward the idea that "embeddings = cached computation." If every session and every agent has to grep and read files from scratch each time, the same understanding work and token cost get repeated over and over; but if you first chunk, embed, and index the code into turbopuffer, an agent can afterward get relevant context quickly through a very cheap query.

On the implementation side, they built a simple CLI: it chunks the code, embeds it with Voyage's code2 model, and writes it into turbopuffer, then gives Claude a search tool that can answer natural-language questions like "how is password reset handled," returning the corresponding code snippet. The speaker specifically stresses that the chunking strategy is actually the hard part: ideally, "an unfamiliar but skilled engineer looking at just this one chunk could tell what it does, why, and how" — for that reason, some companies use an LLM to auto-generate documentation for uncommented functions before embedding them, to improve semantic quality.

To evaluate whether wiring this into Claude Code actually made things better, he used the public ContextBench benchmark. Rather than looking at whether the task ultimately gets solved, this benchmark checks whether the agent reads the human-annotated "golden files/golden lines" while solving the problem. He picked 50 questions that don't directly mention file names or function names, and compared three setups: vanilla Claude Code; Claude Code plus windowed grep (reading at most 50 lines at a time); and windowed grep plus a semantic search tool.

Along the way he found something interesting: Claude really doesn't like using new tools. Even after he added documentation and hints for when to use semantic search, Claude still instinctively grepped wildly and read whole files. Forcing it in the system prompt to "always use semantic search first" actually made performance worse, because semantic search isn't the best tool for every query — the key question becomes when to use which tool.

In the data, precision — how much of what gets read is golden files/lines — did improve markedly thanks to windowed grep and semantic search: vanilla Claude sits at about 65% precision, and adding windowed grep plus semantic search brings it close to 90%. Intuitively, that means vanilla Claude wastes tokens reading irrelevant files about a third of the time; adding windowed grep brings that down to a fifth; adding semantic search on top brings the share of "unnecessary context" read down to about one-eighth.

But recall — how much of the golden files/lines that should be seen actually get seen — isn't nearly as impressive on average: vanilla Claude reads so much that its file-level recall isn't too bad, but it drops off sharply once you get down to the line/symbol level; windowed grep and "windowed grep + semantic search" show roughly similar overall recall, with the semantic version even slightly lower on some metrics. The speaker further splits tasks into "semantic search does better" and "grep alone does better" and finds a clear division of labor: when you need to find "modules/files with similar behavior but different names," semantic search wins decisively; when you need to trace precisely along imports and function names, grep is actually the better tool. This shows the two excel at different types of tasks, and the hard part is training an agent to pick the right one.

The final takeaway: semantic search does improve precision and reduce needless file reads, but the real power is in the combination of tools — grep and semantic search each find different, complementary pieces of code. The long-term winning condition is giving the agent a diverse set of search tools (vector, full-text, regex, attribute filters, etc.) for finding context, and, like Cursor, using methods like RL to train the model to learn which tool to pick in which situation, instead of forcing a single approach through prompting alone. He closes by noting that the simple CLI and example code from this experiment are open-sourced in the Turbo Grep project, and that a more complete guide on "how to do code search on turbopuffer" is coming later.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Make Vibe Coding Safe: How to Test with Playwright

Amazon AGI Lab

✕

This talk explains how to use Playwright to make features built through "vibe coding" reliable and maintainable.

The speaker starts by introducing himself as a former core developer on Microsoft's Playwright, now doing browser automation at Amazon AGI Lab. He frames today's development pattern: people use coding agents like Cursor and Claude Code to "vibe code," quickly producing features that appear to work, but without safety guardrails, real user flows break easily — especially with repeated iteration afterward. The fix is still "write tests," except hand-writing traditional end-to-end tests (Selenium in the past, Playwright now) is too slow and painful.

He then contrasts two approaches. One lets the agent look only at source code, guess what buttons/text will appear on screen, and generate a Playwright test from that. That's fine for small projects, but once you have a large volume of node_modules, third-party packages, monorepo structure, and other complexity, the model gets easily overwhelmed by the code, guesses the wrong locator, and the test fails as soon as it runs, requiring repeated fixes.

The other approach wires the browser and Playwright MCP / Playwright Test MCP into the agent's loop. That way the agent doesn't just look at source code — it can actually open a browser, start a local server, click through the actual screen, and see the DOM and accessibility tree that React/Next.js finally renders. On every click, Playwright MCP feeds the whole accessibility tree (which buttons, labels, text exist) back to the model, letting it use real on-screen information to decide on the most stable locator and generate more reliable tests.

He demos this with a small Next.js "To-do / Console task" app: the goal is to write a test covering a two-step confirmation flow where a delete button requires clicking "delete" and then "sure." An agent that only looks at source code guesses at the UI structure, likely gets the selector wrong the first time, and needs several rounds of error-correction. An agent wired to Playwright Test MCP instead first uses MCP to confirm the server is running, then opens a browser, operates the actual page, clicks delete, and observes whether the list items and counter actually change, before writing a test based on this "real, observed on-screen behavior," and finally calling Playwright test to run it and confirm it passes.

In the middle section, he surveys the current tools that let an agent control a browser/tests: Playwright CLI, Playwright MCP, Playwright Test MCP (with test-runner capability), Vercel's Agent Browser, Google's Chrome DevTools MCP, and so on. The main point: if you're already using Playwright for end-to-end testing, the top recommendation is pairing it with Playwright Test MCP so the agent can directly run tests and manage the test list.

He also recaps the "MCP vs. Skills (CLI)" debate: before late 2025, MCP was token-hungry and inefficient, so people switched to CLI skills for a while to save cost. But Anthropic later improved MCP's execution and context usage, and his own small benchmark shows that MCP now actually consumes fewer context tokens than CLI — so the premise that "you must pick skills/CLI to save cost" no longer holds; in practice, just pick whichever form fits your overall workflow best.

His final practical advice: keep tests "small and sharp," not a pile of hard-to-maintain, bloated suites. For a vibe-coded app, he recommends writing one clear end-to-end spec per critical user journey, so the test suite stays small and green while still covering the flows that truly matter; run it on every commit, and always treat a failure as a bug to investigate, not something to tolerate as flakiness. Pair that with Playwright's screenshot, trace, and video artifacts, so both humans and agents can debug well after the fact. The overall message: building features has become very cheap, but "confidence" is still expensive — so make good use of Playwright plus MCP/agents to build a high-signal-to-noise end-to-end testing workflow, so that fast vibe coding can still ship safely.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Making Neural Networks Smaller: Quantization and Pruning

PrismML

✕

This talk explains how quantization and related techniques can make large neural networks smaller, faster, and more power-efficient, while losing as little performance as possible. Below is a summary of the key points.

Why quantize a model?

The speaker first explains the motivation: you pay for training once, but you pay for inference on every token, every user, every conversation.
Between 2023 and 2025, training cost, while high, grew relatively steadily; from 2024 to 2025, inference cost has already reached the multi-billion-dollar range, and it's still rising fast. Quantization directly lowers inference cost.

Beyond cost, there are two other key pressures:

  1. Memory pressure and deployment footprint

▫ A truly massive model (like Kimi K2) simply doesn't fit on a single GPU at 16-bit precision — it needs to be sharded across multiple cards.

▫ Quantize the weights down to 1-bit, and it can potentially fit on a single H200, greatly simplifying the inference architecture.

▫ For a 7B-class model, after 1-bit quantization it can even fit on a phone.

▫ Prism/Bonsai's example: a 1.7B model, after 1-bit quantization, comes out to about 240MB — extremely small.

  1. Energy and bandwidth bottlenecks

▫ For example, using Int4 (4-bit integer) matrix multiplication instead of FP16 matrix multiplication can cut energy consumption by roughly an order of magnitude.

▫ GPU compute capability grows roughly 60% a year, but memory bandwidth grows only about 20%. If quantization can reduce the number of bits that need to be moved, that's effectively "squeezing more compute out of less bandwidth" — getting a taste of next-generation hardware early.

Don't just quantize the weights: the KV cache becomes a bottleneck too

The speaker specifically warns: at long sequence lengths, the KV cache's memory footprint will exceed that of the weights.

  • KV cache size grows linearly with sequence length.

  • Once context reaches 64K, the KV cache's memory usage is already bigger than the model weights.

  • If the weights are then shrunk further via quantization, the KV ratio gets even more extreme — the KV cache can exceed the weights even at 16K or 32K.
    So when optimizing deployment, you can't only worry about weights — you also need to consider compressing the KV cache or using alternative architectures, such as SSM/Mamba-style models or hybrid architectures. Although these don't yet fully surpass standard attention in performance, they've already been adopted by a large number of the newest models.

What is quantization? Starting with round-to-nearest

He uses an intuitive picture to explain basic quantization:

  • Suppose a neural network layer's weights are distributed roughly like a Gaussian (a bell curve).

  • Build a "grid": for example, 4-bit represents 16 discrete values.

  • Every continuous weight value gets "rounded" to the nearest grid point.

  • The result: what was a continuous distribution becomes a histogram with only a handful of discrete values.

The intuitive effect at different precisions:

  • 1-bit: only +1 and −1 (or ± some constant) — it describes almost none of the shape.

  • ternary (three values): adds a 0, providing a sparsity signal that can express "drop this connection."

  • 2-bit: a bit more signal, but still far from the continuous curve.

  • 4-bit: starts to approximate the bell curve — simple round-to-nearest is usually just about workable.

  • 8-bit: round-to-nearest has barely any issues, with little impact on performance.

One of the biggest enemies: outliers

If all the weights sit within some small range, quantization is easy; but once a few extreme values (outliers) appear, the whole dynamic range gets stretched, and ordinary values get "squeezed" into the same bucket — or even collapse to 0.

Example:

  • A vector where most values sit near 0, with one outlier at 0.95.

  • If a single quantization range has to cover the whole vector, then to accommodate that 0.95, many small values near 0 get quantized down to 0.

  • These outliers are often considered the most important weights in pruning theory, so quantizing them poorly can badly hurt performance.

Fighting outliers: per-group (per-channel) quantization

A simple solution: instead of using one set of quantization parameters for the whole matrix, quantize in groups.

  • For example, for a 16×16 matrix, split each row into 4 groups (4 numbers per group).

  • Each group determines its own quantization range and scale.

  • If an outlier only exists within one group, it only affects that small group — it won't drag down the whole row or layer.

The cost:

  • You need to store one scale per group (possibly FP16 or lower precision), adding a small amount of "extra bits."

  • The speaker gives the Bonzai model as an example: with a group size of 128, storing one extra FP16 scale per group works out to about 0.125 extra bits per weight, so the "1-bit" weights actually come to about 1.125 bits in practice.

  • If the scale itself is also stored at lower precision (e.g., 4-bit), the overhead can be reduced further.

Overall, this is a case of trading a bit more metadata for better quantization results.

Rotations: spreading out outlier energy

Another line of technique is various "rotation" transforms, the most classic being the Hadamard transform.

The intuition:

  • If one dimension of a vector is especially large and the rest are small, multiplying it on the left by a Hadamard matrix spreads the large value's energy across all dimensions, so each dimension only grows slightly.

  • That way, the original single outlier is no longer so extreme, and becomes easier to express with fewer bits.

How this is done in practice without breaking the network:

  • Applying the Hadamard transform directly to the weights would distort the network's function.

  • The common approach: multiply both weights and activations by the Hadamard matrix separately (i.e., add the same orthogonal transform before and after a linear layer), then quantize each separately.

  • Because Hadamard is an orthogonal matrix, applying it and then its inverse on either side leaves the network functionally equivalent, but the piece in between now lives in a Hadamard space that's more amenable to quantization.

  • The core idea: move the data into a coordinate system that's more quantization-friendly, and do your work there.

Mixed precision and SVD-Quant: shifting the hard part and splitting it apart

Next, the speaker discusses "mixed precision":
In practice, when someone claims a "4-bit model," it usually isn't the case that every weight in every layer is really 4-bit. For example:

  • NVIDIA's NVFP4 is actually closer to 4.5–5 bits.

  • Many systems keep critical parts like the output head (LM head) at 8-bit or 16-bit, and only lower the precision elsewhere.

SVD-Quant's core idea

SVD-Quant attempts to quantize both weights and activations to Int4, which is very hard. Its approach breaks down into roughly these steps:

  1. First analyze the activations and discover there are outliers in them — quantizing directly would cause heavy damage.

  2. Do group-based quantization first (per-group, as described above), but it's still not good enough.

  3. Then introduce "smoothing":

▫ Multiply the activations by a diagonal matrix D, turning the original X into X̂, so that X's distribution gets "flattened," making it more quantization-friendly.

▫ Correspondingly, the weights are multiplied by D's inverse, which makes the weights harder to quantize — the problem has been shifted from the activations to the weights.

  1. Then use SVD + LoRA to handle the now-harder-to-quantize weights:

▫ Run SVD on the transformed weights to find the dominant directions (corresponding to the outliers).

▫ Use those directions to build a LoRA branch (the low-rank path L1, L2), and store this "hard-to-quantize part" as a high-precision side channel.

▫ The backbone weights Ŵ can then be quantized to low bits, since the hardest directions have already been split off.

The final result:

  • Activations: become easy to quantize in the smoothed space.

  • Weights: the backbone part can be quantized to Int4; the difficult part is compensated for via the high-precision LoRA branch.

  • The LoRA branch only adds a small amount of memory and a little inference compute overhead, in exchange for substantial overall model compression.

The speaker gives an intuitive demonstration using a "steak picture":

  • The original image is the baseline steak.

  • With methods that quantize only weights or only activations, the steak "still looks like a steak but is distorted."

  • With naive full-Int4 quantization, the image is badly distorted.

  • With SVD-Quant, the reconstructed image with Int4 weights and activations looks almost identical to the original.

This example also highlights that weights and activations, when it comes to handling outliers and quantization, form something like a "duality" — difficulty can be shifted and balanced between the two.

The Bonzai model: a truly "uniform bit-width" 1-bit / ternary model

Many so-called 2-bit or 4-bit models are actually mixed precision. What Prism wants to do is make every layer and every weight the same bit width — no escape hatch.

  • They implemented and released two paths:

▫ A fully 1-bit weight model

▫ A fully ternary weight model (−1, 0, 1)

  • All kernels are open source, running on standard backends (llama.cpp, MLX, vLLM, etc.), and measured on real hardware.

  • Because of the per-group scale, the actual effective bit width is about 1.125 bits/weight.

Performance and memory results

Averaged across a set of six benchmarks (such as IFEVAL, GSM8K, HumanEval, BFCL, MUSER, MMLU Redux):

  • Compared to the full-precision Qwen 3 model, memory requirements drop by roughly an order of magnitude.

  • It still retains about 90–95% of the original performance.

On the "model size vs. average score" Pareto chart:

  • Model families represented by Qwen and Mistral form a frontier.

  • The Bonzai model shifts noticeably to the "left" on this chart, meaning it reaches comparable performance at a much smaller model size — demonstrating the potential of "compressed intelligence."

Speed and energy consumption

Measured inference speed and energy consumption also show clear advantages:

  • On an RTX 4090, for the same model:

▫ The traditional, higher-bit version: about 59 tokens/s

▫ 1-bit Bonzai: about 368 tokens/s (roughly 6x)

  • A similar speedup trend shows up on an Apple M4 Mac as well.

  • On an iPhone, the original precision or the 8-bit version won't fit — you can only use 4-bit; while 1-bit, with a good implementation, can hit tokens/s on a phone that approach a desktop GPU's.

  • On energy: across the 4090, M4, and iPhone alike, the 1-bit version's wattage draw is markedly lower.

Summary

Putting the whole talk together:

  • The main goal of quantization is to reduce inference cost, memory, and energy consumption, so large models can be practically deployed in more places (cloud, edge, phone).

  • Plain "round to nearest" low-bit quantization runs into serious challenges below 4-bit, outliers especially.

  • Techniques like per-group quantization, rotation (Hadamard), mixed precision, smoothing, and SVD+LoRA make up the current main toolbox for fighting outliers and narrowing the "quantization gap."

  • Weights and activations have a dual relationship across these methods, letting you shift difficulty from one side to the other and handle it separately.

  • Prism's Bonzai model demonstrates that, on real hardware, extreme quantization like 1-bit/ternary — with careful design and implementation — can already come close to full-precision model performance across multiple benchmarks, while delivering huge savings in memory, big speed gains, and lower energy use.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Malleable Evals: Why Are We Evaluating Adaptive Systems with Static Tests?

Vincent Koc, OpenClaw

✕

This talk is about why evaluating AI systems that keep changing with "static" evals is no longer good enough.

The talk opens with Vincent starting from the traditional software world: we rely on unit tests, regression tests, CI/CD, plus a bit of chaos engineering and observability to keep systems stable. [1:05–2:48] But when this playbook gets carried over to AI/LLM applications, most people stop at "static benchmarks" and hand-built question sets: manually design a batch of questions, test offline, and if the model doesn't say anything that violates the rules, ship it. [2:52–3:33] The problem is that AI applications, and the contexts they're used in, aren't static at all — yet we're still treating them like ordinary software.

He reviews how LLM development has changed in recent years: early on it was "prompt engineering" — people frantically swapped prompt wording, like blindly trying medications, keeping whatever happened to work. [5:37–6:23] Then came the "context engineering" era, with RAG and tool calling, breaking a large agent down into many small, testable tool modules, and eval started becoming more tractable. [6:27–7:16] From 2025 onward, models got more capable, tokens and the cost of generating code got cheaper, software iteration speed exploded, and it even became possible to use an LLM to solve hard ARC-I2 type visual/pattern problems. [7:22–8:25]

Against this backdrop, he proposes "intent engineering": the machine self-optimizes based on "intent" — for example, modern harnesses (like OpenClaw, and various agent frameworks) try to understand and adapt to you. [8:27–8:49] But as soon as the experience differs from person to person, organization to organization, eval gets much harder: your path and my path are completely different, and the traditional model of "one question set conquers all" simply can't capture real behavior. [8:51–9:14]

So he pushes back on the "evals are dead" claim — he argues the opposite is true: in agentic applications, we need, even more, a multi-layered understanding of what's actually happening inside the agent; it's just that the form eval takes needs to change. [9:15–9:31] The traditional pipeline is: static benchmark → hand-built eval dataset → offline evaluation → after launch, a lack of chaos/real-behavior observation, so when something goes wrong you're back to square one, redoing everything. [9:43–10:02]

He then proposes a new direction: shift eval from "matching the correct answer" to "outcomes aligned with intent." Instead of just asking "does 1+1 equal 2," ask: in an ambiguous, multi-step, multi-tool situation, does the agent match the "style, persona, fuzzy criteria" we actually want? [10:11–10:22] On the research side, you can describe these fuzzy criteria using a rubric (the way you'd grade an art assignment). [10:31–10:39]

He goes on to discuss a few key concepts:

  1. Self-curating test sets from traces: in practice, 80% of usage scenarios repeat in a stable way, but as the user base changes, the shape of questions gradually drifts. [10:50–11:04] If we capture actual execution traces and let the agent build or update its own test set from those traces, it can keep reflecting the latest usage behavior, instead of forever testing a new behavior space with an old dataset. [11:04–11:16]

  2. Online, always-on eval/optimization: eval is no longer just a one-time, pre-launch action — it's an "always-on" service that continuously watches traces, continuously runs agent-based eval, and continuously adjusts. [11:19–11:29]

  3. Telemetry in the loop, a self-healing harness: he mentions his own paper concept — if an agent's harness is aware of telemetry (error rate, cost, latency, etc.) and has some conditions in place (budget, quality thresholds), it can self-adjust and self-repair the moment it detects an anomaly, instead of waiting for a human to step in. [11:34–12:00] This makes the system more like a self-healing agent, rather than a rigid pipeline.

He calls this whole phenomenon "eval calcification": if our evals stay stuck on a fixed dataset and a fixed process, while actual agent and user behavior become faster-moving and more variable, eval itself will calcify — falling further and further behind reality. [12:12–12:27]

As a vision, he cites an idea similar to Karpathy's on auto-research/auto-optimization: you only need to define the goal and the reward signal, and the system explores the optimal configuration in parameter space on its own. [12:31–12:59] For AI applications, eval is no longer a "dataset starting point" — it's the "end state I want to reach" — such as user satisfaction, error risk, or stylistic consistency — and then you let the machine continuously tune parameters and self-correct around that end state. [13:11–13:21]

Finally, he closes with an 80/20 metaphor: 80% of behavior can be covered by fairly static, clearly defined intent descriptions and test coverage, but what will actually wreck your business is that ever-shifting 20%: the weird questions, the unexpected usage patterns, the edge cases. [13:32–13:46] The key is to use an agent to manage that 20%, continuously observing and adapting, and to make eval itself a living agent — one that updates its tests based on traces and self-adjusts based on telemetry, rather than a fixed, unchanging benchmark package. [13:48–14:06]

Overall, the core thesis of this talk is: in the era of agentic, intent-driven AI systems, eval should be designed as "software/an agent that grows and self-optimizes alongside the system," not a static question bank.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

No Dropped Frames: designing a VLM around a latency budget

Moondream

✕

This talk covers how the Moondream team designed an entire system for a real-time vision-language model (VLM), so the model doesn't "drop frames" on a video stream and can run at very low latency on real hardware.

The speaker first lays out the background: traditional computer vision (YOLO, CNNs, etc.) works, but every task needs a whole ML team and expensive labeling, so it's only viable for a handful of high-value use cases. The new generation of VLMs offers a great developer experience — you just describe the task in natural language — but mainstream VLMs today are "too slow and too expensive," often taking several seconds per frame, which can't keep up with applications like robotics or camera tracking that need 30 FPS.

He then draws a clear line between typical LLM inference engines and vision workloads. General engines (like vLLM) are designed for chat/long-context, multi-GPU, large models, optimized for "prefill + long-sequence decode," and can stream text slowly for a human to read; but a vision application receives frames one at a time, each of which must finish within tens of milliseconds, and it typically has to run on an edge device near the camera, with an extremely low tolerance for latency. If a robot arm stalls for 200ms, it might "miss the catch."

To operate under this kind of latency budget, they made design choices at three layers: model architecture, tokenization, and the inference engine.

On model architecture, they "upgraded" what had been a small dense model into a roughly 9B-parameter mixture-of-experts (MoE) — not to make it huge, but to speed up decode. That's because the real bottleneck during decode is HBM (high-bandwidth memory) weight movement, not compute; MoE activates only a small number of experts each time, reducing the amount of weight that needs to be moved per token at the same VRAM budget. Unlike typical large MoE setups, they don't push hard for even usage across experts, because the goal is "single-GPU, low latency," not distributed multi-GPU training. As a result, the model learned some "skill-specific experts" on its own — for example, object-detection requests frequently activate the same batch of experts, further stabilizing latency.

For tokenization, they use an improved BPE called "SuperBPE," which breaks with the traditional tokenizer convention of always splitting on whitespace, allowing common phrases to merge across whitespace into a single token. This cuts token count by about 20% on general English, and by about 30% on vision-description-heavy corpora. That not only lowers inference latency, it's also equivalent to training on less "text volume" per token, directly saving training cost. In addition, for the common task of "locating an object's position," they designed dedicated grounding tokens and an extra decoder, representing box coordinates with a handful of dedicated tokens instead of emitting verbose JSON (full of numbers and commas). On a dense-detection task involving around 30 objects, Moondream needs only about 100 output tokens, whereas a general-purpose VLM might generate thousands of tokens, driving latency up to the tens of seconds.

On the inference side, they built a dedicated inference engine called Photon. This isn't the result of a single "magic optimization" — it's an accumulation of a bunch of 1–3% improvements. First, they wrote a large number of custom CUDA kernels shaped specifically for their own model, fusing what would normally be many separate pointwise operations in PyTorch (each step reading and writing activations from HBM) into a single kernel — reading in once, chaining several operators together on-chip, and writing back only once, cutting down repeated HBM access. They wrote more than twenty such kernels for this.

He then discusses the problem of "keeping the GPU fed": every decode step needs two synchronization points between CPU and GPU (submitting the next batch of work, reading back results); naively "waiting for the previous step to fully finish before scheduling the next one" creates a lot of tiny gaps along the timeline that add up to a noticeable slowdown. The fix is to run scheduling work in parallel with GPU decode: while the GPU is computing the current token, the CPU has already prepared the batch for the next token, updated the KV-cache mapping, handled constrained decoding, and so on — ensuring the GPU can immediately pick up the next batch the instant it finishes, maximizing continuity.

Interestingly, once the engine itself was fast enough, they found that on weaker CPUs (like the Jetson Orin) the new bottleneck became "image preprocessing is too slow," leaving the GPU idle waiting for frames. They ended up implementing their own faster image resizing and other preprocessing, faster than common open-source libraries, achieving nearly a 1.9x preprocessing speedup on weak CPUs.

To let the model be deployed as close as possible to cameras and robots, they hand-tuned extensively for different hardware: various GPUs across the NVIDIA Ampere, Ada, Hopper, and Blackwell families each get custom kernels, as do edge boards like the Jetson Orin. On Apple silicon, rather than rewrite a second engine in MLX, they hooked their own kernels in through PyTorch's custom-op interface — keeping a single codebase while still achieving quite competitive performance.

He closes with a few concrete numbers: on a B200, for single-request, frame-by-frame inference, it's about 30ms per frame; if batching is allowed, it reaches about 71 requests per second (equivalent to more than two 30-FPS video streams). On a robotics/self-driving-oriented SoC like NVIDIA Thor, it's about 147ms; on a top-spec MacBook Pro, about 4.6 images per second.

Finally, he gives an intuitive demo using Super Mario World. In one evening, he wrote a simple framework that lets Moondream "watch" the game screen at about 30 FPS: a "planner" looks at the screen each time and produces a short-term plan of 10–15 tokens, while a "controller" outputs real-time control commands (like jump, walk, stop) at a rate of 1 token per frame. It still makes mistakes and dies occasionally, but building a vision-plus-behavior agent that can watch the screen and play the game on its own, in just a few hours, demonstrates the potential of low-latency VLMs. This example extends naturally to robotics and camera tracking: planning can update more slowly and asynchronously, while control can act continuously at high frequency and reliably, no longer shackled to expensive, laggy VLM inference.

Overall, the core message of this talk is: to achieve "every machine can see," you can't just chase parameter count and model metrics — you need to redesign everything from model architecture and tokenization to the inference engine and preprocessing, all around the "latency budget," so a VLM can genuinely operate on edge devices, in real time.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Optimizing Model Training End-to-End: A Tiny MoE Case Study

Zach Mueller, Lambda

✕

This video is about how to thoroughly optimize the entire training pipeline of a "small MoE model" from start to finish at home, using relatively modest hardware, speeding it up enough to run two experiments per day.

Video overview

Speaker Zach Mueller (Lambda's head of developer relations) uses an experiment with a roughly 500-million-parameter tiny Mixture-of-Experts model to demonstrate how to compress a pretraining experiment that originally took about 60+ hours down to around 13.2 hours on a home multi-GPU machine, while also discussing why small models and small MoE matter in today's agent era.

The content is roughly divided into three parts:

  1. Why small models and small MoE matter right now

  2. The real-world constraints and goals of pretraining at home

  3. The step-by-step process and lessons learned from optimizing the entire training pipeline

  4. Why "small MoE" and "training at home"

Zach first defines "small, medium, and large" models:

  • Small: under about 35B parameters, trainable on a high-end but affordable single machine.

  • Medium: around the 70B level, usually requiring cloud resources on the scale of 8×H100.

  • Large: 100B+, where a single failed training run starts at several hundred thousand dollars.

He observes that small MoE models have sprung up rapidly over the past year or two (Granite, Liquid, Zephyrus, etc.), for the following reasons:

  • The agentic AI wave: it requires many fast-reacting, low-latency models to support large numbers of agents.

  • Local/edge computing: running "hundreds of small agents 24/7" on a Mac, for example, is constrained by RAM/VRAM, forcing the use of very small models.

  • Customizable, bring-your-own-data: small models place less pressure on VRAM, so you can train and fine-tune them yourself without touching the cloud.

  • Small models train fast: going from zero to a demo-worthy pretrained model can be done on the order of days.

But he also emphasizes that optimizing training for small models is different from doing so for large models:

  • Large models are often sharded across many GPUs, so the bottlenecks are mostly "communication" and distributed architecture.

  • Small models mostly fit on a single GPU, so the bottleneck shifts to the data loader, CPU-side overhead, algorithm choices, and so on.

  • Small models saturate on tokens easily, so data quality and distribution must be tightly controlled to make effective use of the limited parameters.

  1. The real-world constraints and goals of pretraining at home

What Zach wants to do is "real pretraining research at home," while keeping cloud costs to a minimum.

His hardware setup and constraints are roughly:

  • One personal workstation equipped with 4 Blackwell RTX 6000 MXM-class GPUs.

  • No NVLink or InfiniBand — the GPUs are connected only via PCIe (and an older generation at that, roughly half the speed of PCIe 5).

  • Electricity isn't cheap, but compared to renting cloud GPUs over long stretches, it's still much cheaper in the long run.

After discussing it with colleagues at Hugging Face, he settled on a practical threshold for the experiment design:

  • Target time per pretraining experiment: about 12–13 hours, so that two experiments can be run per day.

  • Each experiment should run at least close to Chinchilla optimal:

▫ Rule of thumb: token count ≈ 20 × (model parameters in billions)

▫ For example, an 8B model should see roughly 160B tokens (epochs can repeat; what matters is the total token count).

Because:

  • If an experiment takes 2–3 days, it becomes extremely hard to iterate, and it wastes limited compute.

  • Hitting this pace within limited MFU (Model FLOP Utilization) and VRAM naturally constrains the model to a small scale.

He specifically explains MFU:

  • At major labs, on H100-class setups, an MFU of 50–55% is considered good.

  • At home, without high-speed GPU interconnects, stably reaching 30–35% MFU is already ideal.

  • Mixture-of-Experts is inherently "inefficient" in terms of FLOPs utilization — many large-scale experiments get only around 30% MFU; xAI once revealed that some architectures achieved only 11% MFU, simply because the design wasn't aligned with the hardware.

  1. Tiny MoE Case Study: compressing 61 hours down to 13.2 hours

3.1 Problem setup and the "depression calculator"

He chose a small MoE with a "LLaMA-3-like architecture" for the experiment:

  • Keeping the original vocab design, total parameters were reduced to about 500M, with about 330M active (a large portion being embeddings).

  • The goal was to use this tiny toy model to practice the entire pretraining-from-scratch process, while observing which optimizations actually help.

He wrote his own "depression calculator":

  • You input the model size, target MFU, number of GPUs, tokens per batch, etc., and it estimates the time needed to train from scratch to Chinchilla-optimal.

  • Under his home GPU setup, training a 500-million-parameter model to Chinchilla-optimal was estimated to take just over 13 hours — that's about the threshold of "barely acceptable."

  • If you tried to run a 70B model to a similar token count on the same machine, it would take months or even years — completely impractical.

3.2 Initial baseline: 61 hours, with neither GPU nor CPU kept busy enough

The initial baseline setup was roughly:

  • Applying FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel, the PyTorch version), but without aggressive optimizations like FP8 yet.

  • Using BF16, a batch size of 12, and about 200 total batches — a small experiment of roughly 10 million tokens used to extrapolate the full run.

After profiling, he found:

  • The full experiment was projected to take about 62 hours.

  • GPU utilization was around 70%, but CPU overhead was significant, and there was a large pink block in the profiler labeled "other."

  • Time actually spent doing matmul was only a small fraction; much of the time was stuck at the CPU or driver level — data loading, the optimizer, and so on.

3.3 Single-machine optimization: compressing 61 hours down to about 43 hours

He began using the profiler to chip away at bottlenecks one by one:

  1. Adjusting batch size to a power of 2
    The original batch size of 12 was the maximum that "just barely avoided OOM," but Nvidia GPUs tend to make better use of hardware when the batch size is a power of 2.
    He adjusted the batch size to something closer to a power of 2 (like 8 or 16), immediately gaining a modest speedup — only a fraction of an hour's improvement, but helpful over a long run.

  2. Switching to a faster attention implementation (Flex Attention)
    After asking colleagues at Hugging Face, he adopted a more efficient attention kernel.
    The result was a nearly "free" 30% speedup, suggesting the old attention kernel was quite inefficient for this kind of small model/setup.

  3. Addressing the data loader and CPU overhead

▫ First, he used PyTorch's ‎⁠pin_memory=True⁠, reducing the cost of repeatedly allocating host memory for each batch — a small speedup that also shrank the "pink blob" in the profiler.

▫ Next, he pre-tokenized the entire dataset upfront and stored it as a Hugging Face Dataset, so training only needed simple indexing, with no more on-the-fly tokenization.

▫ This step drastically cut CPU-side overhead — the pink block nearly disappeared, and GPU kernels finally became the real star of the show.

  1. Switching to the fused AdamW optimizer
    He asked a PyTorch engineer and was advised to enable the new fused AdamW, which merges what used to be many separate multiply and update operations into a small number of fused kernels.
    After enabling it, that mysterious pink overhead in the profile nearly vanished entirely, leaving less than 1%.

Taken together, the estimated single-GPU training time dropped from 61 hours to about 44 hours — roughly a 50% improvement in throughput.

3.4 Multi-GPU scaling and the communication bottleneck

After optimizing the single-GPU case, he moved on to multi-GPU data parallelism:

  • In theory, with 4 GPUs and high-speed interconnects like NVLink, you'd expect close to a linear 4× speedup.

  • But in his actual home PCIe 4 environment, he only got about a 2.6× speedup, because all-reduce communication was too slow.

Profiling revealed:

  • In the multi-GPU setup, about 50% of step time was spent on all-reduce (gradient synchronization), with only about 20% actually spent running matmul.

  • He estimated:

▫ Each all-reduce takes about 0.08 seconds, which could only be roughly halved under ideal PCIe 5 conditions.

▫ Accumulated across the 100,000-plus batches needed to train to Chinchilla-optimal, communication alone would consume about 2.26 hours.

  • This is a problem large cloud data centers with NVLink/InfiniBand barely need to worry about, but at home it becomes the main bottleneck.

3.5 Using gradient accumulation to cut communication frequency → 13.2 hours

After consulting Eli Barco at Hugging Face, he adopted a common rule of thumb:

  • Most pretraining research designs things so that "each optimizer step processes about 1 million tokens."

  • A single physical batch couldn't fit that many tokens (it would OOM), but gradient accumulation could be used instead:

▫ Assuming each small batch is about 100k tokens, you'd accumulate over 10 steps before doing a single all-reduce and optimizer step.

▫ This cuts communication frequency to one-tenth of the original.

Real-world results:

  • All-reduce dropped out of the top bottlenecks in the profile, its share of time falling noticeably.

  • The estimated time for the whole run dropped straight to about 13.2 hours, hitting his original goal of "running two complete experiments per day."

  1. Conclusions and takeaways

Zach offers a summary and a few reflections:

  1. The overall time went from about 61 hours down to 13.2 hours, not through any single silver bullet, but through a series of detailed optimizations:

▫ Sensible batch size settings

▫ Using an efficient attention kernel

▫ Moving tokenization and data loading out of the training loop as much as possible

▫ Using a fused optimizer

▫ Making good use of gradient accumulation to reduce communication overhead

  1. Not every optimization that helps on a single GPU is worth it on multiple GPUs:

▫ He tried writing a custom CUDA kernel to skip certain matmuls, which should theoretically have been faster.

▫ But for a model this small, the overhead of launching the kernel itself canceled out the gains, and the result wasn't actually any faster.

▫ In the world of small models, a lot of "flashy" optimizations don't necessarily pay off.

  1. Pretraining at home is an option that has only recently become practical:

▫ Once hardware prices and GPU performance reach a certain sweet spot, a personal workstation can run genuine architecture experiments, not just fine-tuning.

▫ Electricity plus hardware amortization can actually be cheaper than long-term cloud compute rental if you experiment often.

  1. Future plans:

▫ Over the next few months, he plans to keep working on this kind of tiny MoE, pairing it with synthetic data and different data blends from providers like Nvidia, and release a batch of small models to explore just how far high-quality/synthetic data can push small MoE.

Overall, this video is a very concrete case study: it demonstrates how to use a profiler and a series of experiments to tune a training task that originally took "three days at home" down to something completable "within half a day," and explains why small, efficient, locally-trainable MoE models are becoming increasingly important in the agent era.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Powering Agents with Context Graphs & Ontologies

Datalinks

✕

This video introduces how Datalinks uses "tabular graphs" and ontologies to give AI agents memory, traceability, and a queryable context layer — a "context graph."

The speaker opens by noting that although everyone talks about graph data and ontologies, real-world enterprise data mostly still lives in tables, spreadsheets, data warehouses, and lakehouses. Traditional graph databases tend to hit walls on performance and complexity once node counts reach the billions, or when multiple graphs need to be stitched together. So their approach is to keep the scalability and familiarity of tables, but build links "cell to cell," connecting everything into one giant "tabular graph," and then layer ontology and graph semantics on top.

The overall pipeline works roughly like this: unstructured documents such as PDFs are fed into a parser and first extracted into raw tables, then a module called "auto model" simulates a data engineer — based on the user's prompt, it automatically identifies corresponding entities and fields such as "product, ingredient, specification, test result, marketing copy," produces a schema, and ends up with a clean wide table. Entity alignment and field-to-field linking follow, forming a cell-to-cell graph. This isn't limited to structured data — in their example, they also tabularized about 400,000 rows (roughly 2GB) of Reddit cosmetics discussion data and connected it into the same graph, to simulate a customer service scenario.

Because the core is still tabular, they can keep traditional analytics operations (filtering columns, group by, aggregation) while also being able to "hop" between tables — for instance, jumping from certain "complained-about ingredients" to the formulas containing those ingredients, then to the finished products, then to the Reddit conversations complaining about those ingredients. The agent uses a query language on this graph that resembles a general-purpose programming language, rather than a specialized graph language like Cypher/Neo4j — so even small models can understand, generate, and execute the queries, and at lower cost.

In the live demo, he used a cosmetics company as an example: first tabularizing 130 cosmetics formula PDFs, then connecting them to 400,000 rows of Reddit discussion. The agent can be asked in natural language to "find the ingredients people are complaining about"; the system first parses the question through the taxonomy, then automatically generates a query, and the result gives not just "which ingredients are complained about" but can also handle a follow-up like "find 5 users who complained about these ingredients and write an upsell message for each one, recommending our own products that contain these ingredients" — while the UI shows every query step, which tables it hopped to, and which rows and columns were used. The speaker emphasizes that this ability to "trace back exactly what specific data the agent used to produce its answer" is the embryonic form of a context graph.

Midway through, he addresses two key questions: first, "how to model the data," and second, "how to keep the agent's context from being drowned in tokens." He believes the bigger challenge is actually the former: how you partition data objects, define each table's columns, and decide which columns link to which — this determines whether the agent can reliably find the right data, rather than repeatedly falling into a loop of "sorry, I can't find the data." His modeling principle is: each table should be "sufficient on its own to build an executive dashboard," and column names should be semantically clear to both ordinary people and LLMs; tables should be "wide and somewhat redundant," but each table should have standalone value, connected into a graph through a handful of linking columns, preserving a structure humans can intuitively understand. That way, the agent only needs to see the schema plus link descriptions to plan its own queries and hops.

He then moves on to the recently popular concept of the "context graph": beyond having a "context layer" (the queryable knowledge graph described above), you also need a graph dedicated to storing the "agent's decision trajectory." This includes what questions the agent asked, what data it queried, what queries it ran, what intermediate reasoning it produced, and what actions it ultimately took. This matters greatly for heavily regulated industries (finance, accounting) or scenarios requiring accountability tracing, because companies want a clear "chain of decision evidence" for the agent during audits or when something goes wrong, shifting some of the responsibility away from individual people and onto an "inspectable system process."

In this section, he shows how to save an entire agent execution trace (questions, queries, results, reasoning text, etc.) as a text file and feed it back into the same auto-model pipeline, this time with a prompt along the lines of "I want to build a context graph that traces agent decisions." The system automatically proposes a set of objects (queries, reasoning_steps, ingredients, complaints, etc.); after you review them, it extracts these entity relationships from the trace and turns them into a new graph. This context graph becomes a "decision graph" that can later be queried — for example, "load all the data and reasoning used in this agent run, then present it in a more digestible form for a human reviewer to check." He notes that an undesigned raw trace is an information explosion for most users, and a well-designed UX and abstraction layer are essential to understanding what the agent is actually doing.

In the Q&A, someone asked how the ontology he uses differs from the traditional Semantic Web / OWL approach. He said he personally isn't fond of heavyweight semantic layers, and rarely sees OWL genuinely deployed in production outside academic teams. His definition of ontology is more pragmatic: it's simply a set of "interconnected objects," each useful in its own right, while also being searchable to find related neighboring entities. The formal logical axioms and taxonomy structure underneath aren't the key thing for most business users — what matters is being able to "search from a particular car instance all the way to its last maintenance record, then jump to the parts that were replaced," not knowing how many axioms are maintaining consistency behind the scenes.

Also in this session, someone asked: since the extraction and alignment process is itself stochastic, and links produced at different times vary slightly, how precisely do humans actually need to verify ontology relationships? Or should they just care about output quality? He replied that in practice, different people naturally take different data paths to solve the same problem, so their goal is less about pursuing a single, uniquely correct graph and more about "having the system propose links automatically at scale, then giving humans or agents tools to sample, adjust, and prune." Whether to let the graph evolve as new data comes in, and whether to auto-prune old links, has to be designed per specific application scenario — there's no universal best answer.

The last few questions centered on data lineage and standards such as OpenLineage; he admitted that Datalinks' lineage is currently in an internal format, but said they would look into these open standards. Someone also asked whether they perform entity resolution and indexing during lineage generation — he answered that they build an index for every cell, and these indexes underpin the cross-table hopping and trace queries shown earlier.

Overall, the focus of this talk is on using a "tables-first, graph-semantics-layered-on-top" architecture to solve three things at once: enabling agents to reason and retrieve reliably over real-world tabular data; letting both humans and agents collaborate on an understandable ontology and schema; and using context graphs to fully record an agent's decision trajectory, supporting auditing, accountability tracing, and further optimization.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Pricing AI Agents

Orb

✕

This video explains how to "engineer" pricing and usage-based billing in the age of AI agents, rather than treating pricing as a financial decision that only gets revisited occasionally.

What is this video about?

The speaker is from Orb, a company that builds usage-based billing infrastructure. They work daily with SaaS and AI product teams, helping design the full "event → metering → billing" system that supports constantly evolving pricing strategies.

They first lay out the broader context:
Pricing used to be a decision the finance department made once a quarter. Now, under AI and usage-based models, pricing has become a decision that product and engineering need to iterate on weekly, and it touches engineering, finance, sales, product, and other departments all at once. If the billing system isn't flexible enough, even a small price change turns into a cross-departmental "relay race" that slows down innovation.

Next, they use four time scales to look at how companies think about pricing:

  • When a product just launches (days/weeks): pricing focuses on "driving adoption," commonly using free tiers, free trials, and freemium.

  • On a scale of weeks: the focus shifts to COGS (cost of goods sold), especially whether the underlying foundation model costs still make sense, and whether free and self-serve users remain profitable.

  • On a scale of months: repricing for "growth," balancing adoption against paid upgrades, and making sure new features and engineering investment get paid for.

  • On a scale of years: designing an overall monetization strategy for "profitability and exit path" — for instance, whether to lean more enterprise, and how to repackage and restructure plans.

They also break down the four major levers involved in pricing:

  1. Price point: exactly how much to charge per unit.

  2. Value metric: what do you actually charge for? Tokens, credits, workflows, outcomes, etc.

  3. Billing model: prepaid vs. postpaid, and whether there's a committed volume.

  4. Contract structure: discounts, adjustments, renewal incentives, and how much flexibility to give sales.

Key concepts and case studies behind successful pricing

From interviews with numerous AI/SaaS companies, they distilled four success factors:

  • Signal capture: how do you know if the pricing is working? This includes community sentiment before and after a price change, customer interviews, revenue pressure, competitor pricing, and actual churn and adoption rates. They especially emphasize: don't just look at how loud the complaints are — check whether there's actual churn and whether usage has dropped.

  • Risk awareness: striking a balance between "flexibility" and "predictability." Finance teams actually like usage-based plans with commitments, since that makes budgeting and risk control easier.

  • Value story: every price change should come with a clear ROI story, telling customers why this change represents more value.
