  1. Metadata and file formats (Iceberg, Arrow, Parquet)

At the metadata and storage layer, they're moving toward open formats:

  • Metadata:

▫ for highly streaming, high-churn data, they still heavily use their own custom metadata system, because the open-source world isn't yet well-suited to this kind of live-edge, continuously-written scenario.

▫ but for "static or slowly-changing" data, they lean toward adopting Apache Iceberg, uniformly managing partitions, snapshots, and statistics for the query engine to use for pruning and planning.

  • In-memory format:

▫ using the Arrow columnar format across the board, so as to enable:

⁃ efficient random access;

⁃ exchanging data between nodes and between systems at low serialization cost.

▫ one layer up from that, there's also Arrow Flight (transport protocol), Flight SQL (query protocol), and ADBC (something like a columnar version of JDBC).

  • Storage format:

▫ historically there were quite a few in-house formats, but they're gradually converging more datasets, and even legacy products, toward Parquet.

▫ a standard storage format makes it much easier for future, unanticipated systems, tools, and models to reuse this data.

Overall, they divide the various components into two broad categories:

  • Contracts: Substrait, Arrow, Parquet, Iceberg, etc., used to "define boundaries" so different components can be swapped in and out.

  • Components: DataFusion, Calcite, etc., providing extensible default implementations, paired with their own specialized implementations.

Results and current progress

This deconstructed stack is still "migration in progress," but some clear wins are already visible:

  • Semantics are now consistent across platforms, and semantic bugs have dropped substantially.

  • Having a unified way to express query/schema/metadata is what makes it realistically possible to build a genuine "semantic layer" at the company-wide level — which is especially critical for AI and agents.

  • Using open-source engines and formats improves resource efficiency, and also saves a lot of time on "commodity" functionality (like general-purpose operators, scanners, etc.).

  • Once the system is broken down more finely into horizontal layers, you can mix and match open-source and proprietary components at each layer, adjusting at a finer granularity and moving in smaller, faster steps.

  • For newcomers, the learning cost drops a lot: getting familiar with Arrow, Parquet, DataFusion, Iceberg, etc. is enough to get started, rather than first having to understand a pile of mysterious in-house custom systems.

Future work and the connection to AI/agents

Finally, Julien lays out several key directions they're headed next, basically all tied to AI and higher-level data products:

  1. Evolving from plain compaction to more general materialized views:
    not just merging small files into big ones, but building smart aggregation, sampling, and pruning structures to support a broader range of more complex query patterns.

  2. Deeper integration between interactive queries and ETL:
    the goal is for the interactive engine and the batch ETL layer to share the same semantics and logic (e.g. both built on Substrait + the same semantic layer), reducing the fragmentation between the "online query" and "offline ETL" worlds.

  3. Unifying the table format and stronger interoperability:
    as more products move toward batch/analytics-oriented use, they need their streaming-first DNA to smoothly connect with the need to "look back over the past several days/weeks for large-scale analytics."

  4. Semantic layer and data lineage:
    when you have a stack of transformation layers and derived datasets, you need to fully track where the data came from, how it was transformed, and what its sensitivity and retention rules are, and carry this semantic information along with the data.
    For AI agents, just seeing the schema isn't enough — they also need to know the semantics of identifiers, what the canonical id is, permitted uses, and so on.

  5. Supporting AI model training and inference/agents:

▫ training: needs to provide consistent, controllable, cost-reasonable access to "all data"; this requires reducing data fragmentation.

▫ inference/agents: letting agents safely read, write, and operate on production systems — doing automated debugging, deployment, rollbacks, and so on — requires the data platform to be complete enough in both semantics and governance.

In summary, this talk is about:
Datadog's evolution, within a massive multi-tenant cloud environment, from a traditional monolithic database, to custom in-house engines, to now building a "deconstructed" database using open standards and composable open-source components.
The goal is: without giving up control over platform details, to make the system easier to evolve and better able to support new kinds of analytics and AI/agent workloads.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

The End of the Internet As We Know It

Raffi Krikorian, Mozilla

✕

This video is an interview with Mozilla CTO Raffi Krikorian at AI Council, centered on what he wrote in a New York Times op-ed: "the truce on internet security is over."

He first explains what the "truce" was: in the past, writing software was hard, and finding vulnerabilities was also hard — the difficulty on both sides was roughly comparable, so even though there were plenty of bugs, they were exploited at scale relatively slowly, creating a kind of time-lag safety buffer. But once models like Anthropic's Mythos appeared, they simultaneously made "writing code easier" and "finding zero-day vulnerabilities easier," and that balance broke. Critical open-source components (ffmpeg, OpenSSL, curl, etc.) often have only one or two maintainers, yet they underpin the entire internet's infrastructure — in a world of AI-automated vulnerability scanning, they're drastically under-resourced, which effectively exposes the whole network to risk at once.

He disagrees with the optimistic view that "AI will fix AI itself, so there's not much to worry about," because the sequencing is inverted: mass discovery and exploitation of vulnerabilities comes first, and patching and hardening only catch up later — it's that long stretch in between, the "vulnerable window while things are wide open," that's the truly dangerous part.

He then turns to the shift in how software gets developed: AI is no longer just a tool, but will increasingly become a "co-author." In practice, engineers already treat the model as a teammate, giving a 400-line AI-generated diff only a quick skim, with the real attention going to tests and behavior. So he argues development culture and tooling need to change: merges should rely more on "behavior and evals" rather than just reviewing the raw diff, and git itself should evolve to record "prompts, model version, eval results, and provenance," so we know which model, in what training state, produced which change.

On engineering management and organizational culture, he uses Mozilla as an example: the Firefox team is very conservative, treating AI as an assistive tool and letting only senior engineers touch critical core code; the Mozilla AI team sits at the opposite extreme, with entire codebases produced almost entirely by models. He's deliberately tolerating this "multi-track experimentation" and some amount of mess for now, relying on a regular technical council to share experience and gradually converge on best practices. He also suggests the industry treat models as "software interns" — imposing boundaries on them, forbidding them from touching critical sections, and requiring clear version and provenance records.

Discussing security and business models, he criticizes how many SaaS companies today treat security capabilities like SSO as a "paid premium feature," arguing security should be the default, not a premium. He advocates moving toward "secure-by-design" — for example, more aggressively adopting languages like Rust, and thinking through how to migrate legacy systems to safer architectures. Open-source maintainers are critical infrastructure here, and he compares them to "essential workers" during the pandemic, arguing that large AI/cloud companies should funnel real money and engineering time back to them, or the whole ecosystem's foundation will collapse.

He then discusses the economics and philosophy of open source and open models:
historically, projects like Linux and various open-source projects in the ecosystem relied on a model where companies "serve themselves while also giving back to the community" — partly for recruiting, partly for marketing and culture, and partly genuine technical reciprocity. He believes the model era needs something similar but taken further, because open weights alone aren't enough — you also need transparent data, evaluation, and post-training processes, otherwise there's no way to understand these models' ethical or decision-making leanings (for instance, he mentions research finding that Western and Chinese models give completely different answers on kidney-transplant decisions).

He also emphasizes that "open doesn't need to conquer 100% of the market" — what matters is having a trustworthy, genuinely usable open alternative in existence. The mere existence of Firefox/Signal forces other browsers and communication protocols to raise their privacy and encryption standards. Likewise, if competitive open AI models and applications exist, they can put pressure on the big platforms, pushing the whole industry in a direction more favorable to users — even though most people will never actually look at the source code.

On large-model interfaces, he points out that the shift from "Google's ten blue links" to "ChatGPT giving you one answer" is hugely significant. Users used to roughly understand what they were choosing between; now it's much easier to hand decision-making entirely over to the model — and the model has commercial incentives behind it (for instance, research finds many answers skew toward platform-sponsored results), which represents a new kind of risk to human autonomy and to the structure of our information diet.

He holds cautious optimism about "whether open models can stand on their own economically": currently, some of the best open-weight models are largely sponsored by big companies, at enormous cost. Companies releasing open-source projects in the past often had recruiting and community-building motives, and that motivation should still exist for models going forward — but it will take more innovative business structures and full transparency of data/models before this can genuinely become a viable alternative to closed models.

Taking the longer view, he doesn't want to see "seven AGIs" — he wants to see "seven billion AGIs": everyone running an agent on their own device that's aligned with their personal values and data, without that data needing to be centralized with a single big company. Achieving this requires solving the developer-experience problem for open models (a default setup as good to use as "the MacBook of the AI world"), breaking the compute monopoly (escaping a single GPU vendor and a closed software stack), and building fine-grained mechanisms for data authorization and provenance.

Later he talks about talent and education. He predicts that by 2035 there will be far more "builders" than there are today: through "vibe coding," people without an engineering background will also be able to build systems; his own 10-year-old son is an example — using an AI IDE to make a bunch of small games, and then, in turn, becoming motivated to learn a real programming language to understand what's underneath. He believes we'll end up redefining the difference between "engineer" and "builder," with experience and taste becoming more central to engineering value: people who understand architecture and have learned from past failures will still have a huge edge when collaborating with models, because they're better at judging what output "looks right but will actually blow up."

At the same time, he worries that if everything becomes too "frictionless," it will erode the "productive struggle" that learning requires. Learning has always been about building judgment through making mistakes, correcting them, and accumulating scars, whereas today's tools tend to pave over every pothole for you. He feels the next real battleground is education: figuring out how to let kids make full use of AI while still preserving enough challenge and frustration for them to learn to think and take responsibility, rather than becoming completely dependent on whatever single answer the system hands them.

Overall, this conversation is, on one hand, a warning that "AI has thrown the internet security equation out of balance," and, on the other, a call to action: re-fund open-source maintainers, design new engineering workflows that treat AI as a team member, build genuinely usable open models and toolchains, and start now designing a "some friction, but real growth" way of using AI for the next generation — so we don't end up handing too much control over to a handful of platforms and black-box models before we've even figured out what's happening.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

The Future Is Domain-Specific Agents

Justin Schroeder, StandardAgents

✕

This video argues that the key to future AI that's actually useful and deployable lies in "composing multiple small, domain-specialized agents" rather than one giant all-purpose agent.

What does he mean by "domain-specific agents"?

Justin first defines "agent":
a piece of deterministic code used to steer the non-deterministic output of an LLM or similar model toward achieving some clearly defined goal. [2:23]

He observes that nearly every company today, even small insurance agencies and realtors, is trying to build its own "custom agent" — not because they lack access to AI, but because they want to properly integrate their own data and processes into an AI system. [4:34]

But building an agent yourself is actually very hard:
you have to handle orchestration of the agentic loop, a provider abstraction layer, durable execution, observability and debugging, environment differences, portability and reusability, and so on — and the result is usually that you get stuck at the demo stage. [5:15–7:32]

The current mainstream approach — tools, MCP, skills — is really all just "inheritance"

He reviews the common integration approaches today:

  • MCP: right now it's mainly become a distribution mechanism for feeding tools to a large-model agent, and in practice hasn't yet delivered much value on other fronts. [8:13–8:55]

  • Skills: essentially just a bunch of markdown documentation. A small number are useful, but research shows that using too many skills makes agent performance worse. [9:24–9:38]

He sketches out a typical agent stack:

model → system prompt → tools → skills → MCP → conversation history [10:08–10:35]

Most of this really just turns into "context" — you keep piling information and capabilities onto the same one agent. He says this closely resembles inheritance in traditional OOP:
you take an object and keep bolting on properties and capabilities until it turns into a giant monster. [12:11–12:29]

This is fine at first (loading up 5 skills), but once you're up to 100 or 1,000, returns diminish and it becomes very hard to control and reason about. [13:04–13:23]

This is why he uses "Composition over inheritance" as the lens for critiquing the current MCP + skills integration approach.

His proposed alternative: domain-specific agents (composed multi-agent systems)

Instead, he argues for composition:

instead of cramming everything into one big agent's context, build a complete but small and specialized agent for each domain, for example:

  • a Figma agent: its system prompt is designed entirely around Figma, containing only Figma-related tools and a very small message history. [13:35–14:14]

  • Gmail, Travel, Sheets, GitHub... each is its own independent agent, with its own agent loop, tools, and state. [14:21–14:38]

Above these sits a coordinator/primary agent, responsible for:

  • deciding when to call which domain agent

  • the agents only communicate with each other using "natural language" (the way a human team writes emails or talks to each other). [14:41–15:21]

He uses the Apollo moon-landing team as an analogy:
what actually got people to the moon was many expert teams collaborating, with each individual skilled at just a few tools and tasks, not one person charging in alone with a giant pile of tools. [15:27–16:17]

This "specialized small agents + collaboration" pattern is what he calls domain-specific agents.

Why is this better? (performance, cost, security, scalability)

He shares his own implementation experience at StandardAgents, arguing that domain-specific agents have several clear advantages:

  1. Token efficiency and cost

Because:

  • each small agent's context is very lean, keeping only the system prompt, tools, and small amount of messaging that the task actually needs.

  • the coordinator only needs a single sentence to invoke a domain agent — for example, "get me Debbie's latest email" — and that's the entire context for that call. [17:30–18:01]

Their actual observation: task-level token efficiency can exceed 80%, and it's also easier to define clear task scope. [16:52–17:10]

On top of that, you can use smaller, cheaper models (even non-LLMs, like diffusion or image models) to handle specific subtasks, so cost can drop dramatically. [18:06–19:10]

He gives an example: the per-task cost gap between some small models (DeepSeek V4 Flash) and top-end models (Fable 5) can reach 137x;
with domain-specific agents, letting the small model handle only what it's good at, with a minimal context, gets you a cost advantage of this magnitude without sacrificing result quality. [18:16–18:37, 18:52–19:10]

  1. Security and permission control are easier

Right now everyone is using "one giant coding agent" to do everything, which ends up bypassing a lot of security and permission mechanisms, because a single agent can, in theory, do anything. [19:30–19:52]

If you switch to many small agents instead:

  • the Gmail agent can only touch the Gmail permissions and API you've allowed it to touch.

  • the Travel agent can only book tickets and look up itineraries.

  • the GDPR agent can only check compliance issues for EU customers.

Each agent's innate capability is inherently limited, so even if the LLM "wants to misbehave," it can't do much beyond its boundary at the tool level. This puts a company's IT/security staff much more at ease. [19:58–20:24]

  1. Better scalability

Each agent:

  • is an independent, small execution environment that can scale horizontally in the cloud, running thousands of instances in parallel around the world. [20:26–20:54]

  • doesn't need to share a giant global state, and doesn't need a huge monolithic VPC.

The whole system ends up looking like a multi-service network of microservices, except the nodes are "intelligent agents."

His predicted timeline: 2027 will be "the year of multi-agent orchestration"

Right now these domain-specific agents don't yet exist at scale in the public domain, but StandardAgents is already using them heavily internally. [21:00–21:19]

He makes a few timeline predictions:

  • Starting in the second half of 2026: you'll clearly see more people talking about domain-specific agents, along with the frameworks, tools, and ecosystem forming around them. [21:19–21:52]

  • 2027: will be the breakout year for multi-agent orchestration, and the term will become a mainstream buzzword. [21:55–22:03]

An important backdrop driving this is:

the belief that "the cost of intelligence keeps falling" has actually, temporarily, reversed in 2026. [22:39–22:52]

He says they track token prices on their website:

  • even after normalizing for "per unit of IQ," token cost has still risen about 29% this year.

  • Ignoring IQ and just looking at raw token price, the increase has actually reached 76%, and the year isn't even half over. [22:44–23:38]

So:

  • long-term (10-year), cost per unit of intelligence will probably still fall;

  • but in the short term, token costs keep rising, forcing everyone to look for cheaper-but-good-enough options.

  • You can't just put an expensive model like Fable in front of every customer as frontline support, unless customer lifetime value is genuinely off the charts. [23:54–24:07]

Domain-specific agents plus small models become one of the only viable architectures that's both "good enough to use" and "affordable." [24:09–24:16]

What does his ideal "complete agent" look like?

In the second half he breaks down what an "ideal agent" should be made of:

  1. The tool layer, broken down into:

▫ functions: actions that genuinely produce external side effects (writing a file, etc.). [24:39–24:49]

▫ prompts: can be a sub-prompt that calls another LLM or model — for example, the main agent uses GLM5.2, while one of its tools uses Nano Banana specifically for generating images. [25:01–25:21]

▫ other complete agents: that is, using a domain-specific agent itself as a tool. [25:23–25:33]

  1. hooks:

▫ allow injecting or modifying message history, or triggering side effects, within the agent's flow.

▫ example: the LLM itself doesn't know what time it is, so you can insert an artificial message into the history — "someone asks the time → a tool replies it's 6:45pm" — to make the LLM feel like it knows the current time. [25:35–26:13]

  1. agent rules:

▫ behavioral constraints including the agent's maximum allowed number of turns, tool-use validation rules, and so on. [26:27–26:53]

  1. file system and sandbox runtime:

▫ every agent should have its own sandboxed file system, similar to how ChatGPT/Claude can generate files for you today and save them. [27:01–27:35]

▫ there should also be sandboxed code execution, letting it write code, run it, and check the results, without directly touching the host OS, to avoid leakage. [27:37–27:54]

Wrapping all of this together is, in his view, what makes a "truly complete" domain-specific agent. [26:55–27:56]

A multi-layered, recursive sub-agent structure

In this architecture, "agent as a tool" means you can:

  • have the top-level coordinator control a Salesforce agent.

  • have the Salesforce agent in turn call a Google Workspace agent to handle spreadsheets and Gmail. [28:20–28:51]

  • have the Salesforce agent separately call an "asset-generation agent," which itself wraps multiple models (image, SVG, etc.) and does its own self-reflection and QA. [28:52–29:15]

  • have the coordinator additionally maintain an entire legal-team agent, which further splits out sub-agents dedicated to compliance checks, like a GDPR agent or an OSHA agent. [29:19–29:47]

As a result:

  • each layer only needs the domain context it actually cares about

  • collaboration happens through natural language plus clearly defined API/tool boundaries

  • the whole system still keeps a small context, high observability, and easy changeability

This is the concrete picture he has in mind for multi-agent orchestration. [29:51–30:04]

Closing: StandardAgents' position and an invitation

Finally, he says StandardAgents is currently:

  • building and refining this domain-specific agent ecosystem internally,

  • beginning to open up early access to a small number of ambitious enterprises looking to adopt small, specialized agents. [30:08–30:27]

He hasn't formally launched a product, but he provides a website and contact info, inviting teams interested in "small, specialized agents collaborating" to join.
The core message of the whole talk is: stop thinking in terms of one giant, everything-plugged-in "god agent," and instead, the way the industrial revolution did, break intelligence apart into collaborating specialized machines and small teams — using composition to actually put AI to work on the production line and in front of customers.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

The Modern Data Stack Lost the War: Stop Building more DataFrame APIs

OpenAI

✕

This video argues: stop putting faith in yet another new DataFrame API, and instead adopt "function-centric data tools" to serve AI agents.

The speaker starts with the evolution of data tools: from spreadsheets, to the data engineers/data scientists of the 2010s raised on frameworks like Pandas, to today's AI agents driven by natural-language interfaces. Many companies today are building "data agents": prompt plus context, handing things to a model that calls a code-interpreter-like tool to produce results. But using a "10 + 32" example, he points out that running the full agent pipeline is actually slow and expensive — far less efficient than a human just writing a small snippet of Python and running it locally.

He then reviews why humans love data frames so much: the tabular structure is intuitive, the API is composable and readable, and it's far easier to understand than complex SQL, letting us process data iteratively. But across the worlds of Pandas, Polars, and PySpark, everyone is really doing very similar things — just with different API styles and performance characteristics. In the past, "which API feels more human" was the deciding factor; but in the LLM era, the readability advantage of an API erodes, because models can generate and translate these APIs themselves.

So if you're going to build yet another new DataFrame library today — whatever kind, a "video dataframe," a "geo dataframe," or "rewritten in Rust" — the focus shouldn't be what the API looks like, but rather: are we using the correct abstraction? His proposed answer is to extract the core logic into simple, reusable Python functions (user-defined functions, or UDFs).

He points out that traditional data frame systems mostly bolt on UDF support as an afterthought, with optimization concentrated on the framework's own column expressions, so performance collapses as soon as you rely heavily on UDFs. There's a benchmark experiment in the video: using 12 UDFs to process a 10GB CSV on a standard machine, comparing Pandas, PySpark, and Polars on single-threaded versus multi-core performance. The conclusion: some frameworks scale with core count and some don't, and "plain Python code" sometimes even beats these frameworks' performance in UDF-heavy scenarios.

He argues that redesigning the engine from a "function-first" perspective can massively optimize performance while preserving ordinary Python syntax and function style: single-threaded execution alone can be an order of magnitude faster than plain Python, and it can scale effectively and near-linearly across cores — the demo numbers go from roughly 144 seconds down to 26 seconds on a single core, and then to about 4 seconds across multiple cores. In other words, truly treating functions as first-class citizens, combined with a good query engine, can give you a full order-of-magnitude performance advantage over the existing data-frame ecosystem.

Going further, once the core abstraction becomes "Python functions + a UDF engine," you can keep the same API while swapping in different execution engines underneath, or even lean on a large model to automatically generate a "translation layer." He shares an experiment: using the existing API plus a UDF-first engine, then spinning up a few Codex instances and designing a simple eval, the model produced, within a single day, a translation layer that used to take the industry 20 people and 2 years to build. This shows that focusing on the right abstraction (functions + engine) can let AI "rewrite" massive amounts of infrastructure for you.

In summary, his conclusion is: in the AI/agent era, data tools should shift from "yet another new DataFrame API" toward a data-oriented code tool built on "high-level operators + shared Python functions + an efficient UDF engine." Functions are like Lego bricks for agents: composable, testable, evaluable, reusable across an entire organization, and able to win on both performance and productivity through a powerful underlying engine plus automatic AI translation. He closes with a gaming metaphor: this kind of data-centric code tool doesn't replace the large model — it's more like the "mushroom" in Super Mario, a power-up that makes agents stronger while saving money — and he calls on everyone to "stop building more DataFrame libraries, and start building data-centric code tools for agents."

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

The Mythical Agent-Month

Posit

✕

This video is about "why software hasn't gotten better now that agents write the code," and tries to answer what a good engineer's value still is in a world where humans barely need to write code by hand anymore.

The speaker starts by saying he was actually a long-time AI skeptic, and it was only after using coding agents — going almost a year without genuinely "hand-writing" much code — that he began seriously thinking about what's still left for humans to do in software engineering. His conclusion: understanding the scope of a problem and its system, design and architecture skill, and "taste" — judging what makes software good — these three things are what matter, and always have. Typing out code by hand was always hard labor for humans; we just had no way to skip that "labor" before. Now that agents can write it for you, the genuinely hard part stands out even more.

He then borrows the classic concepts from Fred Brooks' The Mythical Man‑Month and "No Silver Bullet" to look at the "agent era":
Brooks splits complexity into "essential complexity" (the real problem the system has to solve, the core functionality needed, and the correct abstractions) and "accidental complexity" (the busywork that comes from languages, frameworks, build tools, CI, test frameworks, boilerplate, interacting with databases, and so on). Agents are extremely strong at handling accidental complexity: you can tell Claude or another model to refactor the entire test architecture, swap frameworks, cut boilerplate, or change languages, and they do it all quickly. But on essential complexity, they struggle to discern what actually matters, which domain model to use, how to layer things, or what the correct MVC/component logic behind a UI should be. If you don't hand them a clear design and abstraction up front, they'll produce a "matted hairball" of code — it runs, but it's loaded with technical debt, and every subsequent round of a new agent session just piles another slab of meat onto this Frankenstein's monster.

He compares this situation to the "tar pit" from The Mythical Man‑Month: it used to be masses of human engineers struggling in the tar; now it's a pile of parallel coding agents rampaging through different git worktrees, stepping on each other, causing endless rebases, conflicts, and rework. The only difference is that technical debt now accumulates at "machine speed" — problems that used to take a big system one or two years to develop can now show up in one or two weeks.

He also revisits Brooks' law — "adding people to a late project makes it later" — and the idea that a "small, elite surgical team beats a large team," which leads naturally into Conway's law: system architecture ends up mirroring organizational structure. A small team can maintain "conceptual integrity," making the whole system look like it came out of one head; a large team splits into many subsystems, which increases coordination cost and creates inconsistent boundaries. Now, if you throw a pile of agents at a large existing codebase without anyone responsible for maintaining the overall design, the result is, in practice, just as bad as it used to be with a large human team.

For greenfield projects, using an agent today to spin up a new repo, build the scaffold, and set up the build system feels amazing — it's got that "god's-eye terraform" rush to it; but he points out that once these projects go through a few hundred or a few thousand agent sessions and accumulate a few hundred thousand lines of code, they quickly turn into brownfield projects on their own. At that point, constrained by LLM context size and architectural limits, it becomes increasingly painful for agents to understand and refactor the whole system — even piling on design docs, a memory layer, and tool calls only slightly eases the underlying constraint.

This leads him to a key observation: those of us who used to write code as "individual contributors" are, in effect, all turning into software architects/product managers. Our main job has become "saying no": when an agent proposes some implementation or design direction, you have to be able to spot what's wrong with it and have the nerve to hit the brakes, demanding a more sensible abstraction and design instead. If you lack this kind of technical judgment and taste, and just keep saying yes, the system will rapidly turn, at the agent's high output rate, into an unmaintainable monster.

On the skills side, he talks about his own transformation: after 20-plus years of writing code, becoming fluent in Emacs, refactoring techniques, quickly navigating large codebases — traditional "hands-on" skills — almost none of it gets used now. What he thinks matters more going forward:
being able to establish "conceptual integrity" without writing much code, being able to clearly visualize the rough shape of a problem and its solution;
being able to "spell out" clearly to an agent, in the correct design vocabulary (architecture, abstraction, module boundaries, quality attributes), what you actually want;
being able to read and understand an existing system's design, and write useful specs and constraints based on that understanding.
So engineers and students will, going forward, need to spend more time reading code and studying architecture and design, rather than just practicing hand-writing coding problems.

He then talks about his actual "agentic engineering stack" and some tools he's built himself. On one hand, he makes heavy use of spec-driven development frameworks like Superpowers — first clarifying requirements, deciding on the design, and forming a detailed spec, then handing it off to a whole set of implementation agents to grind through. This workflow places human value at the "design and review" stage rather than the typing stage; he'll even kick off a plan that takes 14 hours to run, hand it to an agent to execute, go to sleep, and check the output the next morning.

On the other hand, he's also found that relying on these frameworks alone still produces code with plenty of bugs and quality issues, so he started building his own "automated code review and observability" system:
he mentions Robo Rev: every line of agent-generated code gets reviewed at least once, usually four or five times, cross-checked by different models and tools, with every review result going into a "ledger" that must be explicitly closed out — nothing disappears into some ephemeral conversation. Over a few months, he's run more than twenty thousand code reviews through this system.
He also built a session database that stores all his coding agents' sessions in one place, supporting full-text search and analysis of tool usage and token consumption, so he knows "where my money would actually be going if I were paying by the API."
Because the GitHub web interface is unstable and has a bad experience, he simply built his own GitHub UI (a middleman) — it uses GitHub's API but with an entirely different interface, and that's where he normally reviews PRs, merges, and reviews.
He also built his own issue tracker (Coda), storing issues and their state in SQLite, so agents can record their understanding of a problem and their progress within a structured system, instead of scattering markdown everywhere.
On top of that, he uses some parallel-agent management tools (like superset.sh, which he uses) to help manage twenty or thirty git worktrees at once, letting agents run linters, fix PRs, and so on for him.

On language choice, he says he made a clear pivot in early 2026: moving away from a Python-centric world — cutting Python almost entirely — to a Go-centric one instead. He wrote an article discussing "human ergonomics" versus "agent ergonomics":
Python is great for humans to read and write, which was also his motivation for building pandas back in the day;
but for agents, what matters more is whether compiling and testing are fast, whether deployment is easy (a static binary you can drop straight onto a server), and whether dependencies and the runtime carry overhead (something like the JVM looks heavier by comparison these days).
So languages like Go and Rust — which produce static binaries, compile fast, and deploy simply — turn out to have better "ergonomics" for agents. In a world where languages and frameworks are highly replaceable and get quickly rewritten by agents, a system's truly lasting value sinks down more into the lower-level, hard-to-replace core systems (things like database engines, DuckDB, and the like), rather than the choice of application-layer language on top.

Looking ahead, he acknowledges on one hand that models keep advancing rapidly — a step up every six months, agents doing more and more with fewer and fewer bugs, code correctness improving. On the other hand, he stresses that even so, essential design, scoping, and taste are problems that are hard for models to solve automatically. As things stand, the people who can truly push agents to the limit and produce high-quality software are still those engineers with deep technical background and architectural experience. He doesn't believe future models will fully replace these roles.

Finally, he brings all of this back to "taste and responsibility":
there's a huge amount of poor-quality, frustrating software out there in the world (he names Gmail's search experience directly), and behind it is often a product or design side that doesn't know what "good" looks like, and can't tell whether what they've built is actually bad.
In a world where "generating code is nearly free," the old excuse of "we don't have enough engineering resources" no longer holds up — the only problems left are really just two: you don't know what a good product looks like, or you can't recognize whether the thing in front of you is good or not.
He encourages people with design ability and taste to see this as an opportunity: look at all the terrible software left over from the pre-AI era, treat it as a target ripe for replacement, and use agents plus a new engineering stack to build a better version yourself.

For him personally, he's gone from a skeptical, veteran tool author to someone who's fully embraced "agentic engineering" — burning through a billion tokens a day, working seven days a week, an experimenter generating and reviewing huge volumes of code across multiple languages. He enjoys building tools while, at the same time, feeling his way toward new development workflows, and he hopes this talk reminds people that what truly shapes the quality of future software isn't who can type fastest, but who can maintain a system's conceptual integrity, design it properly, and have the courage to say "no" to an agent.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

The Open Layer: How Open Models, Routing, and Inference Are Reshaping Agentic Engineering

Panel: OpenRouter, Fireworks, Arcee

✕

This video is a panel discussion at the AI Council SF '26 conference, on the theme of: in the world of "agentic engineering," what role do open-source/open-weight models, routing, and inference infrastructure play, and what are the practical engineering trade-offs.

What is the video about?

The moderator first sets the scene: everyone's talking about closed frontier models (like OpenAI, Anthropic), but in practice, a lot of "long-running agents" and new applications are built on a hybrid technology stack: open-weight models, multi-model routing, and high-throughput, low-latency inference infrastructure.

A few founders from OpenRouter, Fireworks AI, and Arcee AI then answer several core questions from different angles:

  1. What does "open" actually mean now?

Several definitions at different levels are put forward:

  • From OpenRouter's perspective, "open" means optionality and substitutability:
    it's not about using a bunch of models today — it's that you know your needs will change in the future, so you want to be able to switch models or providers at any time, rather than being locked to a single closed API. It also includes openness of data, such as publishing model performance and usage data to help the ecosystem collectively adjust and train better models.

  • From Arcee's perspective, "open" is more of a bet on people and community, rather than a bet purely on IP/software itself:
    the model weights, training recipe, and code are open, but the real asset is the group of people who can keep training the next generation of models, plus the users who will take these things and innovate with them. So "making the secret sauce public" isn't really giving up IP — it's expanding the community and accelerating iteration.

  • From Fireworks' perspective, "open" is broken down in a more technical, institutional way:
    there's a distinction between "weights-only" and "truly open source," plus many dimensions:

▫ whether the training code is open

▫ whether the training data or its sources are disclosed

▫ what license it's released under (research-only vs. commercially usable)
some people are now even building an "openness index" to quantify exactly how open different models actually are.
Even weights-only releases (with no open training recipe) are still extremely valuable to the ecosystem, because they provide control and extensibility.

  1. Open vs. closed: what are the downsides and risks of using open-weight models?

Everyone on the panel is genuinely very "pro-open," but they still honestly discuss a few real-world considerations.

  • The dynamics of capability and cost:
    many open-weight models today are already "good enough" for most everyday tasks and are an order of magnitude or more cheaper, but the closed frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic) command enormous compute, and are likely to open up a large gap on the hardest, most cutting-edge capabilities in the future. This leads to a situation where:

▫ on the real commercial work you actually care about, closed models are only a little better, yet far more expensive.

▫ so the real challenge becomes figuring out which scenarios truly require a closed frontier model versus which ones an open-weight model handles just fine, rather than treating it as a black-and-white either/or choice.

  • A safety perspective (using Anthropic's argument as an example):
    some people (Anthropic in particular) genuinely believe that as model capability increases, fully open weights bring safety risk, so "having clear control over the weights" is part of protecting humanity. You may agree or disagree, but this is indeed one important voice in the conversation today.

  • Provider variance:
    in the open-weight world, the exact same model weights can sit behind wildly different inference systems. The real quality difference is often not a surface-level issue like "quantized or not," but that whole large layer of software in between: scheduling, concurrency, batching, caching, GPU utilization, and so on. The result is:

▫ the exact same model can feel completely different across different inference providers.

▫ some FP16 services can even be worse than some FP4 ones, simply because the system engineering is poor.

  • Chinese open-weight models and geopolitics:
    quite a few of the most competitive open-weight models right now come out of China, and this touches on several dimensions:

▫ their training corpora include a large amount of the "Chinese internet," which is fundamentally different from the data available to US models, and may reflect different viewpoints on sensitive topics like politics and history.

▫ many enterprises have a gut-level unease about "using Chinese software," even though model weights are, at bottom, just numbers.

▫ certain applications (e.g. political communication, value-sensitive domains) worry more about this kind of bias; but for general coding and everyday tasks, most panelists think it's not a big problem.

▫ what genuinely worries some US-side groups is that if "open weights" end up dominated mainly by China, the US would have almost no leverage on safety standards, regulation, or governance, leaving only the extreme option of "outright banning" them.
At the same time, some point out that these models' answers, across different languages, reflect the viewpoints of different societies — which is, in its own way, a kind of "mirror."

  • Regulation and data provenance:
    legal action (a number of cases from 2023–2024) has made a lot of companies that want to use models care a great deal about: whether the training data is properly licensed, whether its sourcing is clear, and where the model was trained.
    Some "domestic" open-weight teams (like Arcee) specifically emphasize:

▫ being able to clearly tell customers the distribution and proportions of their data sources.

▫ this is an important selling point for compliance and risk management.

  1. Why have cost, speed, and psychological perception become so critical?

Midway through, the discussion shifts to "cost and pricing models" — a part that matters a great deal to practicing engineers and product people.

  • Changes in cost structure:

▫ models keep getting stronger, but the per-token price has an upward trend.

▫ agentic workflows (agents) mean "a single task" can consume a huge number of tokens, even running for a long time in the background, so an organization can easily burn through a whole year's budget in six months.

▫ the key metric is no longer just "price per token," but how much it costs and how long it takes to complete an entire task.

  • The cost advantage of open-weight models:
    the panelists repeatedly emphasize that for a huge number of real commercial tasks, open-weight models are already good enough, at an order of magnitude lower cost. If you're just generating slides or automating general business processes, there's likely no need to pay 5x the price for "a 50% boost in frontier math ability."

  • "Good, fast, cheap" — pick your trade-off:
    Fireworks proposes a three-dimensional trade-off:

▫ Quality

▫ Cost

▫ Speed
different tasks have a different optimal point among these three:

▫ frontier research problems may only care about quality;

▫ a lot of internal enterprise applications just need to be "good enough" and will prioritize minimizing cost;

▫ for interactive interfaces, where a person is sitting in front of the screen waiting for a result, speed has a huge effect on the experience — for example, 200 tokens/s creates that "feels like magic" difference.

  • Agents turn "speed" into a background cost:
    even for a background agent, where no human is watching the screen waiting for a result, speed still matters, because:

▫ you're paying for an entire workflow — the sandbox, external APIs, surrounding compute, and so on.

▫ if the model is too slow, even though you save on inference cost, you waste other resources and time.

  • User psychology and pricing:
    there's a section using a "watching the gas-pump meter" analogy that's remarkably precise:

▫ users staring at token-based pricing feels like watching the numbers tick up on a gas pump — it creates a lot of psychological pressure, producing a kind of "range anxiety."

▫ pure usage-based pricing gives individual users a bad experience; a lot of people much prefer a subscription model (like Netflix), which feels a lot less stressful.

▫ for people selling a product, separating the "product subscription fee" from the "inference bill" also helps with communication:

⁃ you can say: "our product's monthly fee is X, and separately, there's an overall inference bill across the whole company, which we'll help you optimize — but that's a separate tab."

⁃ rather than having users see "the more you use, the more you pay" on the same bill and become reluctant to use the product.

  • Exploring pricing models:
    the video mentions an experiment Fireworks ran called a capped "Firepass subscription," which the community responded to very well, suggesting that a subscription with a reasonable usage cap, combined with usage-based pricing beyond that cap, might be a viable middle path.
  1. Routing and "which layer controls everything"?

The final section discusses, in the architecture of the future, "who decides how to route to which model, on which hardware," and which layer can genuinely "get fat" and capture the value.

  • A multi-layer "cake":

▫ at the bottom are the GPU/hardware vendors.

▫ above that are the efficient-inference platforms (e.g. Fireworks).

▫ above that are the multi-model routing, unified-API layers (e.g. OpenRouter).

▫ and, higher still, the product companies and agent platforms.

  • Who gets commoditized, and who captures the value?

▫ a business that's purely "I have a lot of GPUs and run public models" struggles to build a moat.

▫ an inference platform capable of custom post-training and helping customers optimize task cost can pull itself toward higher value-add, in a "consultant + infra" direction.

▫ a multi-model routing layer that holds a large amount of usage and performance data can also become the "intelligence hub" for choosing the best model/provider.

  • A possible "bipolar" latency distribution:
    some predict that inference services in the future may split into two poles in terms of latency:

▫ one that's extremely fast, commanding a high per-unit price for interactive experiences;

▫ and one that tolerates slower speed but runs background tasks in a cheaper, batched, ultra-high-utilization mode.
At the same time, specialized hardware like Cerebras and new technologies like Talos will play a role here.

Overall conclusion

The thrust of this panel can be condensed into a few points:

  • In 2026, "open" is no longer just "whether the code is on GitHub" — it's the whole package of control, customizability, optionality, and community iteration speed.

  • In real-world business scenarios, open-weight models often strike a great balance between cost and good-enough quality, and are especially well suited to the multi-step, long-running workflows in agentic pipelines.

  • Closed frontier models will remain extremely strong at the cutting edge of capability, but what actually matters commercially is: which tasks are worth paying that premium for, while everything else gets handled by open weights plus smart routing plus efficient inference.

  • On the engineering and business-design side, the real challenges are:

▫ how to find the optimal point among quality/cost/speed that suits your own use case,

▫ how to design pricing and product experience so users don't get scared off by the "token meter,"

▫ and at which layer (model, inference, routing, or product) to build genuinely sustainable value and differentiation.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

The Prompt is the Platform

Dominik Tornow, Resonate HQ

✕

This video discusses a new software engineering workflow for "designing and producing systems with agents," where the core idea is that the product becomes the specification rather than the implementation, and the prompt itself functions like a platform.

  1. Why "the specification is the product, the prompt is the platform"

The speaker opens by describing a trend: [0:37]
In the past, what we reused was "generic implementation" (libraries, frameworks, platforms); in the future, increasingly, this will shift to:

  • We reuse "abstract specifications"

  • Coding agents then dynamically generate "custom implementations" targeting different infrastructure [1:03]

For Resonate, this means:

  • Their real product is no longer a particular server implementation or SDK

  • The real product is the abstract, reusable "Resonate protocol/specification" [2:05]

  • From that single specification, multiple "target-platform-specialized" server implementations can be generated (for example, a general-purpose version, and versions built in partnership with cloud or infrastructure vendors) [2:22]

So: the prompt (how you describe requirements and specification) becomes the entry point to the platform; the specification itself becomes the reusable core asset.

  1. Why going straight from an abstract spec to an implementation fails

They initially tried to have the agent generate "a Resonate server implemented in Rust + Postgres" directly from the abstract specification [5:11], and the result was:

  • It could only handle the happy path; basic tests passed

  • But it broke under concurrency, process failures, and network failures [5:31]

  • It was essentially more like a prototype than a production-ready system

The reason: the "gap" between the abstract spec and the concrete implementation is too large.
To bridge that gap, they inserted an intermediate artifact: a concrete specification [5:52].

The process becomes:

  • Abstract spec

  • Human-led, agent-assisted authoring of a concrete spec for a specific target (e.g. Postgres): including schema, indexes, SQL queries, transaction boundaries, etc. [6:16]

  • The agent then writes the implementation following this concrete spec [6:28]

While this can produce a production-grade system, it exposes a limitation:
the agent is only "helping implement," not truly "participating in design" [6:40].
If the specification itself is meant to be a reusable, sellable product, that isn't enough.

  1. Moving the agent "upstream": adding a simulation environment

In the second round (targeting NATS as the platform), they reframed the question: [7:04]
instead of asking "can the agent build a production system directly," they asked:

  • What tools does the agent need in order to first design the correct algorithm, before implementing it?

There were two key changes:

  1. Give the agent a repeatable, inspectable, deterministic simulation environment (written in Python) [7:23][12:38]

  2. First have it produce a "simulation implementation," not a production implementation [7:31]

This "simulation implementation" isn't the product — it's "executable design" [7:42]:
it's used to find the correct distributed algorithm under partial ordering and partial failure (concurrency, stale reads, errors) [7:46].
Once the algorithm is confirmed correct in simulation, the agent then derives a "concrete specification" from that result [7:55], and only then comes the production implementation [8:01].

So the new pipeline becomes:
Abstract spec → simulation implementation → concrete spec → concrete implementation [8:06]

In this process, the agent is no longer just "a worker who writes code" — it actually participates in, and even leads, the design. [8:17]

  1. Why a "minimal protocol + simple primitives" is a precondition

For the agent to work within this pipeline, the protocol itself has to be extremely small and simple.

Resonate spent three years continually asking themselves:
"Can this abstraction be removed? Can this property be dropped? Can this relationship be decoupled?" [8:35]
Through repeated simplification, they eventually arrived at a very small protocol built around just two core objects:

  • durable promise

  • durable task [8:56]

Even so, even a "simple concurrent distributed protocol" still has a very complex state and behavior space [9:04], and implementing it on any concrete platform is far from easy — for humans and agents alike.

He then used NATS as an example:
Resonate can only use the handful of primitives NATS provides: queues, a versioned key-value store, and delayed/scheduled messages [9:24].
The design problem becomes: "how do we express the Resonate protocol using only these primitives?" [9:44]

  1. Simulating NATS's consistency model: fresh reads and stale reads

They focused on NATS's versioned key-value store to demonstrate where the real difficulty lies: the consistency model.

  • Most of the time, reading a key gets you the latest value (a fresh read) [10:11]

  • But sometimes you get an older version (a stale read), which is "legal" under that platform's consistency model, not a bug [10:23][10:40]

The system can't only be correct when "the platform's behavior happens to be considerate" —
it has to be correct under all legal behavior [10:52].
This means the simulation environment has to faithfully represent:

  • Possibly a fresh read

  • Possibly a stale read

  • Carrying version information, so you can later determine which "world" you were looking at [11:06]

The key point: at the moment you read, you don't actually know whether it's stale —
you only discover, when you later try to write and fail due to a version mismatch, that what you read earlier was actually the old world [11:21].
Building an "always-correct application" on top of this model is hard for both humans and agents [11:48].

  1. The key to making the agent succeed: deterministic simulation + "forbidden fruit" debug information

The tooling they built for the agent has two layers:

6.1 The deterministic simulation environment itself

They implemented a simulated version of NATS in Python:

  • The key-value store keeps the full version history for every key [12:48]

  • ‎⁠get⁠ sometimes returns the latest version, and sometimes (controlled by deterministic randomness) returns an older version [12:57]

  • ‎⁠update⁠ implements optimistic concurrency control: it only succeeds if the version read is still the latest, otherwise it errors [13:10]

This gives the agent an environment that is:

  • Identical to the real platform in terms of correctness-relevant behavior

  • Yet reproducible, deterministic, and observable [13:23]

So when the agent writes a buggy algorithm, you can replay the exact same failing trace and have the agent fix it for that specific scenario [13:37].

6.2 The "forbidden fruit information" in the simulation

On a real platform, reading a key-value pair only tells you "this value and its version" — it won't tell you whether it's stale, and won't give you the latest value [14:10].
Actual production code can't depend on this information, so it isn't visible in production.

But in the simulation, they allow themselves to record this information:

  • Every ‎⁠get⁠ emits a trace event,

  • recording whether that read was fresh or stale, what value was obtained, and what the "latest value" was at the same time [14:35]

For the algorithm, this is "forbidden fruit": it cannot be used directly to make decisions.
But for the agent, it's extremely useful debug information that can be used to understand and explain "why an invariant broke" [14:49][15:38].

The result is:

  • The algorithm itself only sees legal observations and errors

  • The agent, however, sees a much richer trace, from which it learns:

▫ Not just that "an invariant broke,"

▫ but that "the invariant broke because a decision was made in the stale world" [16:04]

This makes "causality" visible to the agent, enabling it to improve the algorithm in a targeted way [16:08].

  1. The final workflow and conclusion

With this tooling and process, the agent is able to:

  1. First produce a proof-of-concept implementation in the deterministic simulation environment, and validate it through testing [16:22]

  2. From that simulation implementation, derive a concrete specification that is "known to have a correct algorithm" [16:33]

  3. Then generate the real production implementation based on the concrete spec [16:45]

The whole chain becomes:
Abstract spec → (agent-led) simulation implementation → concrete spec → concrete implementation [16:53]

In this way, the agent is no longer just writing code at the very end of the chain — it actually participates in design, and even becomes the design driver [17:01].
The speaker sums it up in one line: the prompt is the platform, the specification is the product, and deterministic simulation is what lets agents truly "move upstream."

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

The World Is Not Enough: RL's Environment Problem

Panel: Fleet, Prime Intellect, Taste

✕

This video is an AI Council panel titled "The World Is Not Enough: RL's Environment Problem," focused on one core question: to make RL / agents stronger, the real bottleneck is no longer compute — it's "high-quality environments."

Main points and conclusions

The moderator opens by noting that ML's bottleneck used to be training data; now it has shifted to how you obtain "environments" that are good enough and diverse enough for RL and post-training.

Several guests (the founders of Fleet, Prime Intellect, and Taste) first define "RL environment": in the traditional RL sense, it's a venue where an agent can take actions and receive observations and feedback; but they emphasize that today's environment types are already very diverse, including tasks with clear ground-truth answers, AI-as-a-judge (scored by a model), and even various evals/benchmarks — all of which can really be seen as a kind of environment.

They argue that the hardest part of environment design today isn't "generating questions" — it's "verification/scoring":
in verifiable domains like coding and math, checking an answer is easier than posing the question, so progress is fast; but once you move into subjective domains (design, aesthetics, creative work, writing style, personality, even scientific research), it becomes a matter of decomposing "fuzzy notions of good and bad" into an operable grading system — one that avoids reward hacking while still reflecting true quality and intent. This requires very deep domain expertise, so "grading/verification" itself becomes the new bottleneck.

They also criticize the fact that many "data/environment vendors" today claim to offer high-quality RL data, when in reality a lot of the environments and data aren't refined, and have limited real effect on capability improvement. Market consolidation is likely: most vendors will be dropped by labs for insufficient quality, and only a few that can reliably deliver environments that actually improve capability will survive.

What makes a "high-quality environment"?

The speakers answer "quality" from different angles:

  • Part of it is quantifiable: in verifiable domains (especially coding), a high-quality environment lets the model make significant progress on an independent eval/holdout test — the reward curve isn't just overfitting to the training questions themselves.

  • But in subjective domains, quality also includes: whether you're genuinely solving the "real problem" you want to solve, whether the environment is close to the real distribution, and whether you've avoided just feeling good about sitting at some local optimum. Many teams see a clean reward signal and conclude the environment must be good — but in reality they may have simply trained the model to be very good at ticking the "correct" box, rather than learning the right behavior.

  • They stress that eval and environment are tightly coupled: if the eval is designed too close to the training environment, you'll be misled into thinking you're doing great (the reward curve goes up), while generalization and real-world experience haven't actually improved. This is also why looking only at public benchmark scores (e.g. coding evals or SweetBench-style benchmarks) often fails to reflect real user preference and "taste."

Taste's founder uses design as an example:
judging "whether a website's design is good" involves basic instruction-following, layout, contrast, and font choice, but also creativity and fit with the target audience (e.g. a finance site vs. an avant-garde brand). Turning this fuzziness into a deterministic training signal usually requires a lot of upfront curation first: working with experts to organize the style space, mapping fonts and styles to audiences — only then do you have a shot at decomposing fuzzy aesthetics into evaluable subtasks that can drive large-scale environments and evals.

Human experts, taste, and the part that "can't be fully automated"

A recurring theme:
high-quality environments can't be separated from truly top-tier human experts, but experts are limited in number, and producing environments/annotations is itself expensive work.

The consensus is that, long term, things will move toward an architecture of "highly automated + human in the loop":

  • Use research and engineering techniques to turn existing data (e.g. production traces) into environments that can supply a reward signal, maximizing the automated share.

  • But wherever things are genuinely subjective, taste-driven, or governed by deep judgment calls, you still need a small but critical set of experts to define standards, provide seed signal, or correct systemic bias.
    They even point out that most users have neither the motivation nor the time to "train their aesthetic sense" — the average user's preference isn't necessarily the target you want. To learn "taste," you should specifically seek out taste-makers, rather than random crowd preference.

There's also an interesting tension here:
if you drive environment and design purely with hard metrics like A/B tests and conversion rate, you easily end up with something that "converts extremely well but looks awful"; if you optimize purely for the highest aesthetics or creativity, it may not serve business goals at all.
Their conclusion is closer to: explicitly model the "goal/intent," acknowledge that multiple equally valid answers exist, and then choose, based on the use case, which one you're optimizing for (mainstream appeal, refined niche aesthetics, conversion rate, long-term brand, etc.) rather than collapsing everything into a single metric.

Real-world data vs. synthetic environments: product and data flywheels

A large chunk of the middle section discusses how to turn real-world usage traces (production data) into RL environments and reward signal.

They argue that, going forward, every software company that wants a moat in the AI era will have to "redesign its product" to capture useful preference and behavioral data, forming its own proprietary data/model improvement loop.
Examples include:

  • Tesla's self-driving: when you disengage autopilot, the car asks you why — a clever way to solicit a reason label from the user at exactly the right moment, unobtrusive yet high-value.

  • Developer tools like Cursor: using an "accept/reject suggestion" UI so users provide preference signal through natural use.

  • Fintech or SaaS companies (like Ramp): deliberately designing features that make "user preference/judgment" explicit as events and labels — something that could not only improve their own models in the future, but potentially even become high-value RL data sellable to large labs.

They also note that if a company hasn't instrumented its product today to produce RL data, it will be in a very passive position later if it wants to build its own models or resist the general-purpose labs — it's likely to get "swallowed."
In the long run, every company will build out its own "research lab + environment-building" capability, the same way companies once built IT departments or ML teams.

That said, they also acknowledge production data has blind spots: for example, in the DoorDash case, what you observe is "someone ordered a salad, delivered in 20 minutes," but you don't know their real goal was "eat something healthy, quickly." Intent is hard to reconstruct purely from logs, so you still need deliberate product design and extra signal — you can't rely on raw usage traces alone.

Longer term: long-horizon tasks, continual learning, and automated environments

The later section on future trends makes several points:

  • The single biggest economic incentive will push massive resources toward agents that can "complete long-horizon tasks and directly substitute for knowledge work" — e.g. programming, law, finance — because they can deliver enormous productivity gains and business value.

  • Coding-style long-horizon environments will remain the most central category, because once you have good enough coding agents, you can indirectly automate any domain by "writing software for that domain" (e.g. writing a tax-filing system, automated contract review, etc.).

  • The real ceiling on capability will most likely only be broken by "deploying in the real world + harvesting production traces back," rather than endlessly self-congratulating inside a closed simulation — similar to how self-driving cars go from simulation to real road testing, then feed failure cases back into training.

  • Under this architecture, "environment-building" increasingly looks like blanketing real economic systems with sensors, observing agent-human interaction, then distilling those traces into a repeatable training environment.

At the same time, they note that environments shouldn't be seen purely as an RL-only tool. Environments can be used to produce high-quality trajectories for supervised fine-tuning, to study and classify model behavior, and to analyze safety risks (e.g. deceptive strategies learned during training) — uses that go well beyond "just the reinforcement learning step."

A few contrarian takes

In the closing quick-fire round, a few "contrarian" opinions worth noting:

  • Someone felt that rubric-style scoring is over-relied on, and actually constrains our ability to express quality and taste.

  • Vincent argues that simply rolling RL out to more economic domains, and continuing to scale RL, is enough on its own to drive us toward AGI — a large part of the model progress of the past few years already came from this kind of post-training/RL expansion.

  • Nic feels the term "RL environment" is itself somewhat misleading, causing people to tie environments too tightly to the RL technique specifically, while ignoring that an environment is really a much broader family of tools — for generating trajectories, analyzing behavior, and building a capability taxonomy — and shouldn't be confined to "reinforcement."

  • He also argues that, compared to a model that can run long, deep reasoning, a model that is "broadly trained across the world's distribution and can answer instantly" is what most people actually want.

Overall, the core message of this panel is:
if the previous generation's key asset was massive raw datasets, then in the "agents/RL era," the key asset becomes "environments" — scalable, verifiable, close to the real world, and able to encode human expertise and taste — along with the whole infrastructure needed to keep automating those environments and feeding them back into the product and model improvement loop.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Towards Reliable Financial Agents: How a 4B Model Outsmarted a 235B Giant

Snorkel AI (with UC Berkeley)

✕

This video introduces how Snorkel AI, working with UC Berkeley's Sky Computing Lab, used a 4B expert model fine-tuned with reinforcement learning to beat a 235B general-purpose giant model on real financial-report analysis tasks — the key wasn't reasoning power, but "tool-use discipline" and reliability.

The speaker first explains Snorkel's research background: they focus on data-centric AI, and have published many benchmarks and open-source tools, of which this work is one. The collaborating team used the open-source RLLM framework to train specifically for the "financial Q&A + tool use" scenario.

The first step of the core experiment was to build the FinQA benchmark. The process has three stages: first, extract close to 7,000 SQL tables and schemas from SEC 10-K annual reports using models and programmatic methods; then, with the help of financial experts, design question types (growth rate, financial ratios, year-over-year change, etc.), and have the model automatically generate question-answer pairs plus structured intermediate information (which fields, rows, and reasoning steps were used) from the tables; finally, apply three layers of verification — programmatic checks (to avoid hallucinated fields/tables), automated review by a proxy model, and item-by-item correction by human experts — to obtain high-quality training and test data.

In this FinQA environment, the agent is given only four simple tools: list table names, look up table info, execute a SQL query, and a calculator. It sounds simple, but large general-purpose models (using Qwen3 235B as the example) make serious mistakes with these tools: they "hallucinate" table names out of thin air instead of using the available get_table_names; they like to fire off careless ‎⁠SELECT *⁠ queries that flood the context with useless data; and when they hit an error, they repeat the same failed strategy instead of correcting based on the error message. These issues also show up in other enterprise scenarios (e.g. insurance underwriting) and in closed-source models (like ChatGPT and Claude).

They then used the RLLM framework to RL fine-tune a Qwen3 4B Instruct base model. The environment is a classic reinforcement-learning loop: the model calls tools and queries SQLite (an in-memory set of 6,900 tables) over ReAct-style interactions of up to about 20 steps, and is finally rewarded via a binary correct/incorrect signal from an "LM-as-a-judge" (GPT-5 Nano). To overcome judge latency, they generated rollouts with roughly a thousand concurrent environments and cached judge calls via Portkey — about 40% of judge calls hit the cache — letting more than 360,000 scoring calls complete on 8 H100s in about 21 hours for under $500.

The training result: this 4B model reached close to 60% pass@1 on the FinQA benchmark, while the 235B version scored only about 51%. In other words, in this well-defined tool environment, the small, specialized model is not only cheaper but also more accurate. Even more interesting: although it was trained only on tasks with "a single table and a unique numeric answer," the "tool-use discipline" it learned transferred to the much harder FinQA Reasoning task — multiple tables (2–5) requiring an open-ended answer with analytical narrative. Here too, the 4B model significantly outperformed both its own baseline and the 235B model.

They also tested "cross-domain" effects by dropping the fine-tuned model onto the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard. The results showed that while there wasn't a big improvement on other general tool-calling tasks, there was no regression from the financial-specialization fine-tuning either — only small improvements on metrics like multi-turn memory — indicating that this kind of specialized RL fine-tuning doesn't destroy the model's original general tool-use ability.

On the training design side, the team ran several ablation studies. The first was on data curriculum: single-table data only, a mix of single-table and multi-table data, and a curriculum that starts with single-table before moving to multi-table. The results showed that once the model truly mastered the three "foundational tool skills" — schema exploration, correct SQL syntax, and error recovery — it could naturally compose them into multi-table capability; the real bottleneck was tool-use reliability itself, not simply feeding it more complex examples. The second was on reward design: comparing pure binary reward, partial credit (giving 0.2 just for querying the right table), and a fine-grained rubric (scoring data extraction, computation, reasoning structure, etc. item by item). The conclusion was that the simplest "0/1 correctness" reward actually worked best, because a complex reward too easily bakes in human bias about "the correct steps," constraining the model's exploration and ultimately suppressing performance.

The video closes with a few practical takeaways. First, for work with a highly fixed process and well-defined tools, like financial-report analysis, "efficiency can beat scale": a 4B expert model's inference cost is roughly 1/10th that of the 235B model, and an estimated 50,000 analysis queries per month can be handled on a single GPU — very favorable economics for enterprise deployment. Second, if your business process has clear tools and fixed steps (finance, healthcare, law, insurance, etc.), building a FinQA-style benchmark plus RLLM-style RL fine-tuning is a reusable blueprint. Third, Snorkel is investing several million dollars through its "Open Benchmark Grants" program to keep opening up more benchmarks, data, and model weights, hoping the community can make "reliable expert agents" — rather than giant generalists that just reason haphazardly — the new normal in real enterprise settings.

From the original talk-notes (embedded in this file) ▶ Source videoClose

Trillion is the New Billion: Managing Really Large Multimodal Datasets for AI

LanceDB

✕

This video covers how LanceDB helps AI teams manage "trillion-scale, multimodal datasets" so that model development doesn't get dragged down by data infrastructure.

The speaker opens by describing the typical pain points of today's multimodal AI (text, image, audio, sensor, video, etc.) development: the same batch of data gets copied over and over across different stages — labeling, feature engineering, training, evaluation — by different teams using different tools, scattered across S3, Iceberg tables, Snowflake, various vector stores, and file systems. The result is large files being shuffled around repeatedly, tables running into the billions or trillions of rows, with search, data filtering, and training each running on separate systems that then have to be joined together with custom code. Small frontier labs end up spending huge amounts of time operating a pile of tools and Kubernetes rather than working on the model itself.

LanceDB's premise is: use one unified data layer to support the several key workloads across the AI development lifecycle (search, scanning and filtering, training, evaluation) instead of building a silo for every step. Technically, there are a few core design choices. First, the same dataset stores both very large "blobs" (e.g. images, sensor sequences) and fine-grained fields together, indexed across multiple modalities, so you can do high-speed slice-and-dice and search jointly across timestamps, lidar, sensor, and location streams, even once tables reach tens of billions of rows. Second, data is immutable and versioned with lineage — every commit tells you who produced which fields and via what pipeline, making it easy to trace back and debug when data goes wrong. Third, they implement what they call "zero-cost schema/feature evolution": adding a feature to a 50PB-scale table doesn't require rewriting the whole table or building a separate silo — every AI engineer can incrementally add columns to the same table and have them immediately indexed and queryable.

The speaker also emphasizes how modern workloads differ from traditional big data: previously the focus was large-scale scan-and-aggregate, whereas now it's much more interactive, complex-conditional querying — especially for things like self-driving and robotics, which need cross-modal, time-aligned queries.
